html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 搜神記 - Classical text title meaning 'Records of the Supernatural,' a collection of stories about spirits, ghosts, and supernatural phenomena.
  • 散騎常侍 - High-ranking imperial official in the Jin dynasty, often involved in advising the emperor and handling important state affairs.
  • 令升撰 - Ambiguous phrase; could mean 'commissioned by' or 'written by' someone named '令升.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty (266–420 CE), a period in Chinese history marked by political fragmentation and cultural flourishing.
  • 新蔡 - A place name that may refer to a specific region or county, but its exact location and significance in this context are unclear without additional historical context.
  • The text '搜神記' reflects the cultural fascination with the supernatural and the blending of folklore, mythology, and historical accounts.

Proper Nouns

  • 搜神記 - Title of the text being referenced.
  • 晋 - The Jin dynasty (266–420 CE).
  • 散騎常侍 - A high-ranking imperial official title.
  • 新蔡 - A place name, possibly referring to a region or county.
  • 干宝 - The author of '搜神記,' a historian and writer during the Jin dynasty.
  • 令升 - A name or title whose exact meaning is unclear in this context.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 考先志于载籍 - Investigate ancient records and texts.
  • 收遗逸于当时 - Gather forgotten or overlooked material from contemporary sources.
  • 非一耳一目之所亲闻睹也 - Not something that can be directly heard or seen by one's own ears and eyes.
  • 卫朔失国，二传互其所闻 - Refers to the conflicting accounts of Wei Shuo's exile in the *Zuo Zhuan* and *Gongyang Zhuan*.
  • 吕望事周，子长存其两说 - Refers to the dual accounts of Jiang Ziya (Lü Wang) serving the Zhou dynasty in *Records of the Grand Historian*.
  • 闻见之难，由来尚矣 - The difficulty of hearing and seeing has been so since ancient times.
  • 书赴告之定辞 - Refers to official documents and their fixed language.
  • 据国史之方册 - Based on the official histories and records.
  • 缀片言于残阙 - To piece together fragments from incomplete sources.
  • 访行事于故老 - To inquire about past events from the elderly.
  • 事不二迹，言无异途 - No double tracks in events, no divergent paths in words.
  • 先贤前儒 - Earlier scholars and Confucian scholars.
  • 分其讥谤 - To share the blame or criticism.
  • 著述 - Writing or composition.
  • 发明神道之不诬 - To demonstrate that the ways of the gods are not false.
  • 群言百家 - Various schools of thought and writings.
  • 耳目所受，不可胜载 - What can be heard and seen cannot be fully recorded.
  • 粗取足以演八略之旨 - To roughly select what is sufficient to illustrate the principles of the Eight Categories.
  • 演八略之旨 - Refers to the principles of the Eight Categories, a classification system for knowledge.
  • 根体 - Essence or core.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 搜神记 - A famous collection of Chinese folktales and legends compiled by Gan Bao during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 晋散骑常侍 - A high-ranking official position in the Jin dynasty, responsible for advising the emperor.
  • 新蔡 - A place name, indicating Gan Bao's origin.
  • 卫朔失国 - Refers to the historical event of Wei Shuo's exile, as recorded in different historical texts.
  • 吕望事周 - Refers to Jiang Ziya (Lü Wang) serving the Zhou dynasty, a key figure in Chinese history.
  • 子长 - Refers to Sima Qian, the author of *Records of the Grand Historian*.
  • 八略 - A classification system for knowledge in ancient China, divided into eight categories.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence contrasts the reliance on written records with the limitations of direct experience.
  • This clause provides examples of conflicting accounts in historical texts.
  • This phrase generalizes the difficulty of obtaining accurate information.
  • This sentence emphasizes the challenges even in official records.
  • This clause highlights the difficulties of reconstructing the past from fragmented sources.
  • This sentence argues that consistency is a challenge in historical writing.
  • This phrase explains why historical writing and study continue despite these challenges.
  • This clause suggests that the author is willing to share the blame for any inaccuracies.
  • This sentence asserts that the work aims to demonstrate the truth of spiritual matters.
  • This clause emphasizes the vastness of knowledge and the limitations of human perception.
  • This sentence explains the scope and purpose of the work.
  • This clause expresses hope that future readers will appreciate the work.

Proper Nouns

  • 搜神记 - The title of the work.
  • 晋散骑常侍 - Gan Bao's official title.
  • 新蔡干宝 - Gan Bao's full name and origin.
  • 卫朔 - A historical figure whose exile is recorded in different texts.
  • 吕望 - Another name for Jiang Ziya, a key figure in Chinese history.
  • 子长 - Sima Qian, the author of *Records of the Grand Historian*.
  • 八略 - The classification system for knowledge in ancient China.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 神農以赭鞭鞭百草 - Literal description of Shen Nong using a red stick to strike various plants, symbolizing his discovery of their medicinal properties.
  • 盡知其平毒寒温之性 - Means 'fully understanding their properties of toxicity, coldness, warmth, etc.'
  • 臭味所主 - Refers to the scents and flavors associated with the plants and their medicinal uses.
  • 以播百谷 - Means 'to cultivate and spread various grains.'
  • 服冰玉散 - Refers to consuming a magical elixir called 'Ice Jade Powder,' which grants immortality or special abilities.
  • 能入火不燒 - Means 'able to enter fire without being burned,' indicating supernatural powers.
  • 隨風雨上下 - Means 'to move with the wind and rain, up and down,' describing a form of flight or levitation.
  • 追之 - Means 'to chase after,' indicating the pursuit of someone.
  • 得仙 - Means 'to attain immortality' or 'to become a deity.'
  • 俱去 - Means 'to leave together,' indicating a shared departure.
  • 積火自燒 - Means 'to accumulate fire and burn oneself,' describing a form of self-immolation that leads to ascension.
  • 好食松實 - Means 'to enjoy eating pine seeds,' indicating a preference for a specific diet.
  • 形體生毛 - Means 'to have a body covered in hair,' describing a physical transformation.
  • 兩目更方 - Means 'to have eyes that are square-shaped,' indicating a unique physical feature.
  • 能飛行逐走馬 - Means 'to be able to fly and chase horses,' describing supernatural abilities.
  • 時受服者，皆三百歲 - Means 'those who consume it live for three hundred years,' indicating longevity.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 神農 - Refers to Shen Nong, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology known as the Divine Farmer, credited with the discovery of agriculture and herbal medicine.
  • 赤松子 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the ability to control weather.
  • 西王母 - Refers to the Queen Mother of the West, a prominent figure in Chinese mythology associated with longevity and immortality.
  • 高辛 - Refers to Emperor Ku, a legendary ruler of ancient China, often associated with the Xia Dynasty.
  • 赤将子轝 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his ability to fly and his skill as a craftsman.
  • 寧封子 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his expertise in pottery and his ability to ascend to immortality.
  • 偓佺 - A legendary figure in Chinese mythology, known for his ability to fly and his preference for eating pine seeds.
  • 槐山 - Refers to a mythical mountain in Chinese mythology, often associated with immortality and the abode of deities.

Proper Nouns

  • 神農 - Shen Nong, the Divine Farmer.
  • 赤松子 - Chi Songzi, a legendary rain god.
  • 西王母 - The Queen Mother of the West.
  • 高辛 - Emperor Ku.
  • 赤将子轝 - Chi Jiangzi Yu, a legendary craftsman.
  • 宁封子 - Ning Fengzi, a legendary potter.
  • 偓佺 - Wei Quan, a legendary figure.
  • 槐山 - A mythical mountain.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 号七百岁 - Said to have lived for 700 years; emphasizes his legendary longevity.
  • 学仙 - Learned immortality; refers to the pursuit of eternal life.
  • 翻然飞去 - Suddenly flew away; describes a swift transformation or escape.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 彭祖 - A legendary figure known for his longevity and wisdom, often associated with Daoist immortality.
  • 殷 - The Shang dynasty; one of the earliest recorded Chinese dynasties.
  • 大夫 - A high-ranking official in the Shang court.
  • 桂芝 - Lingzhi mushrooms, considered medicinal and symbolic of longevity and health in Chinese culture.
  • 祷请风雨，莫不辄应 - The ability to command rain and wind, a common attribute of deities or immortals in Chinese mythology.
  • 啸父 - Shou Fu, a legendary figure known for his mastery of fire.
  • 孔甲 - A king of the Xia dynasty, known for his interest in the supernatural and his support of immortals.
  • 龙师 - A teacher of dragons, indicating a high level of spiritual or magical knowledge.
  • 周成王 - King Cheng of Zhou, a significant figure in Chinese history.
  • 蜀羌人 - A person from the Shu region, which is in modern-day Sichuan province.
  • 绥山 - A mountain in the southwestern part of China, known for its association with immortals and mythical creatures.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Shandong province.
  • 王子乔 - A legendary figure associated with immortality and often depicted as a young, handsome man.
  • 蜺 - A rainbow, often used symbolically in Chinese literature to represent transformation or divine presence.
  • 戈 - An ancient Chinese weapon, often used in rituals or as a symbol of authority.

Proper Nouns

  • 彭祖 - Peng Zu, a legendary figure known for his longevity.
  • 殷 - The Shang dynasty.
  • 大夫 - A high-ranking official.
  • 桂芝 - Lingzhi mushrooms.
  • 祷请风雨，莫不辄应 - The ability to command rain and wind.
  • 啸父 - Shou Fu, a legendary figure known for his mastery of fire.
  • 孔甲 - A king of the Xia dynasty.
  • 龙师 - A teacher of dragons.
  • 周成王 - King Cheng of Zhou.
  • 蜀羌人 - A person from the Shu region.
  • 绥山 - A mountain in Sichuan province.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Shandong province.
  • 王子乔 - A legendary figure associated with immortality.
  • 蜺 - A rainbow.
  • 戈 - An ancient Chinese weapon.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 冠先 - A proper name, likely a person known for his actions.
  • 钓鱼为业 - A common phrase indicating that fishing is his occupation.
  • 得鱼，或放，或卖，或自食之 - Describes the actions taken after catching fish, which can be released, sold, or eaten.
  • 常冠带 - Means he always wore formal attire, indicating a sense of propriety.
  • 好种荔，食其葩实焉 - Indicates his fondness for planting litchi trees and eating their fruits.
  • 宋景公问其道，不告，即杀之 - Describes a situation where the King of Song asks him about his methods, and he refuses to reveal them, leading to his execution.
  • 踞宋城门上，鼓琴，数十日乃去 - Describes how he sat on the city gate, played the qin, and stayed for several days before leaving.
  • 宋人家家奉祠之 - Means that every household in Song worshipped him.
  • 琴高 - Another proper name, likely a historical figure known for his musical abilities.
  • 能鼓琴 - Indicates his skill in playing the qin.
  • 为宋康王舍人 - This phrase means he was a retainer of King Kang of Song.
  • 行涓彭之术 - Refers to a practice or skill, possibly related to water management or purification.
  • 浮游冀州、涿郡间二百余年 - Describes his wandering in the regions of Jizhou and Zhuo Commandery for over two hundred years.
  • 后辞入涿水中，取龙子，与诸弟子期之 - Describes how he later entered the Zhuo River, retrieved a dragon's offspring, and made an appointment with his disciples.
  • 明日皆洁斋候 - Means that everyone should purify themselves and wait the next day.
  • 于水旁设祠屋 - Means setting up a shrine by the water.
  • 果乘赤鲤鱼出，来坐祠中 - Describes how he indeed rode a red carp out of the water and sat in the shrine.
  • 且有万人观之 - Means that there were thousands of people watching.
  • 留一月，乃复入水去 - Means that he stayed for a month and then returned to the water.
  • 陶安公 - Another proper name, likely a historical figure known for his skills in metalworking.
  • 六安铸冶师也 - This phrase means he was a metalworker from Liuan.
  • 数行火 - This phrase means he frequently worked with fire.
  • 火一朝散上，紫色冲天 - Describes a sudden burst of fire that produced a purple flame reaching the sky.
  • 公伏冶下求哀 - This phrase means he prostrated himself in the forge and begged for mercy.
  • 须臾 - This phrase means a short while later.
  • 朱雀止冶上 - This phrase means a red bird (symbolizing the south direction and fire) landed on the forge.
  • 安公！安公！冶与天通 - This phrase is a call to him, indicating that the forge had become connected to heaven.
  • 七月七日，迎汝以赤龙 - This phrase means that on the seventh day of the seventh month, he would be welcomed by a red dragon.
  • 至时，安公骑之，从东南去 - This phrase describes how he rode the red dragon and left towards the southeast.
  • 城邑数万人，豫祖安送之，皆辞诀 - This phrase means that many people from the city came to bid him farewell.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋景公 - Refers to King Jing of Song, a historical ruler.
  • 宋康王 - Refers to King Kang of Song, another historical ruler.
  • 冀州 - Refers to Jizhou, an ancient region.
  • 涿郡 - Refers to Zhuo Commandery, an administrative division.
  • 朱雀 - Refers to the red bird, one of the Four Symbols in Chinese mythology, representing the south direction and fire.
  • 龙子 - Refers to the offspring of a dragon, often associated with magical or supernatural beings in Chinese folklore.
  • 六安 - Refers to Liuan, a place name.
  • 铸冶 - Refers to metalworking, specifically the process of casting and forging metals.

Proper Nouns

  • 冠先 - A proper name.
  • 宋景公 - A historical ruler.
  • 宋康王 - A historical ruler.
  • 琴高 - A proper name.
  • 赵 - A historical state.
  • 冀州 - An ancient region.
  • 涿郡 - An administrative division.
  • 朱雀 - A mythical creature.
  • 龙子 - A mythical creature.
  • 六安 - A place name.
  • 陶安公 - A proper name.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有人入焦山七年 - Literal phrase meaning 'there was someone who entered Mount Jiao for seven years.' Describes a person's prolonged stay in a specific location.
  • 老君与之木钻，使穿一盘石 - Metaphorical action conveying a spiritual or mystical task. 'Laojun gave him a wooden drill and instructed him to drill through a stone plate.'
  • 此石穿；当得道 - Conditional statement linking physical effort with spiritual achievement. 'When this stone is drilled through, you will attain enlightenment.'
  • 遂得神仙丹诀 - Achieving immortality and gaining mystical knowledge. 'He then obtained the elixir of immortality and the secret formula.'
  • 援琴而弦，歌曰 - Musical and poetic element. 'He took a lute and played it, singing:'
  • Lines from the song: 'Bright and clear, the sky shines over the four seas. Knowing I love the Dao, you come down from above. You will grant me feathers to fly. Rising on blue clouds, treading on Liangfu. Seeing the three luminaries, meeting the Big Dipper. Driving the winds and clouds, commanding the jade maidens.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 焦山 - A mountain in Jiangsu Province, China, known for its historical and cultural significance.
  • 老君 - Refers to Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
  • 神仙丹诀 - Refers to the elixir of immortality and the secret formula for achieving it, central concepts in Daoist alchemy and spirituality.
  • 鲁少千 - A historical figure, possibly a Daoist practitioner or a legendary character.
  • 山阳 - A region in present-day Shandong Province, China.
  • 汉文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han (r. 180–157 BCE), a ruler of the Western Han Dynasty.
  • 淮南王安 - Prince Huai of Anhui (d. 122 BCE), a prominent figure during the Western Han Dynasty, known for his interest in Daoist practices and alchemy.
  • 八老公 - Eight old men, often associated with Daoist mythology and the Eight Immortals.
  • 梁甫 - A mythical mountain, often associated with Daoist cosmology.
  • 三光 - Refers to the sun, moon, and stars, important symbols in Chinese cosmology.
  • 北斗 - The Big Dipper, a significant constellation in Chinese astronomy and Daoist cosmology.
  • 玉女 - Jade maidens, often depicted as beautiful and ethereal beings in Daoist mythology.
  • 淮南操 - A well-known piece of music or ode associated with Prince Huai of Anhui.

Proper Nouns

  • 焦山 - A mountain in Jiangsu Province, China.
  • 老君 - Laozi, the legendary founder of Daoism.
  • 神仙丹诀 - The elixir of immortality and the secret formula for achieving it.
  • 鲁少千 - A historical figure, possibly a Daoist practitioner or a legendary character.
  • 山阳 - A region in present-day Shandong Province, China.
  • 汉文帝 - Emperor Wen of Han (r. 180–157 BCE).
  • 淮南王安 - Prince Huai of Anhui (d. 122 BCE).
  • 八老公 - Eight old men, often associated with Daoist mythology and the Eight Immortals.
  • 梁甫 - A mythical mountain, often associated with Daoist cosmology.
  • 三光 - The sun, moon, and stars.
  • 北斗 - The Big Dipper.
  • 玉女 - Jade maidens.
  • 淮南操 - A well-known piece of music or ode associated with Prince Huai of Anhui.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 甚易 - Very easy.
  • 分當萬死 - Should be punished a thousand times to death.
  • 默然忽去 - Silently vanished.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 劉根 - A historical figure known for his Daoist practices and supernatural abilities.
  • 嵩山 - A famous mountain in Henan Province, known for its Daoist temples and spiritual significance.
  • 漢成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han, the ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 漢明帝 - Emperor Ming of Han, the ruler of the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 尚書郎 - An official title in the Han dynasty, responsible for various administrative duties.
  • 河東 - A place name referring to the Hedong region, known for its historical and cultural significance.
  • 鄴 - A city in Hebei Province, known for its historical importance during the Han dynasty.
  • 太史 - The Grand Historian, an official responsible for astronomy, divination, and historical records.
  • 舄 - Shoes, specifically a type of shoe worn by officials in ancient China.

Proper Nouns

  • 劉根 - Liu Gen, a historical figure known for his Daoist practices.
  • 君安 - Jun'an, Liu Gen's courtesy name.
  • 京兆長安 - Jingzhao Chang'an, a place name referring to the capital of the Han dynasty.
  • 漢成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han.
  • 嵩山 - Mount Song, a famous mountain in Henan Province.
  • 漢明帝 - Emperor Ming of Han.
  • 尚書郎 - An official title in the Han dynasty.
  • 河東 - A place name referring to the Hedong region.
  • 鄴 - A city in Hebei Province.
  • 太史 - The Grand Historian, an official responsible for astronomy, divination, and historical records.
  • 舄 - Shoes, specifically a type of shoe worn by officials in ancient China.

[8]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 不知所从来 - Common phrase meaning 'no one knows where he came from,' indicating the mysterious origin of the character.
  • 斗酒片脯 - Traditional offering of a small amount of wine and a bit of dried meat, symbolizing modest but sincere hospitality.
  • 示致微意 - Polite expression meaning 'to show a small intention' or 'to convey a slight sentiment.'
  • 饮啖终日不尽 - Vivid description meaning 'drinking and eating all day without finishing,' emphasizing the miraculous nature of the event.
  • 遁去 - Euphemistic term for 'disappearing' or 'vanishing,' often used to describe someone leaving mysteriously.
  • 摩挲 - Term meaning 'to rub' or 'to stroke,' often used to describe a tender or affectionate gesture.
  • 适见铸此 - Phrase meaning 'just now saw it being cast,' referring to the recent casting of the bronze figure.
  • 小住 - Polite request meaning 'to stay a little longer,' used in a friendly context.
  • 迟徐 - Term meaning 'slowly' or 'leisurely,' often used to describe a calm and unhurried demeanor.
  • 走马不及 - Hyperbolic expression meaning 'even a galloping horse cannot catch up,' emphasizing extreme speed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蓟子训 - Legendary figure in Chinese folklore, known for his mysterious appearance and miraculous abilities.
  • 东汉 - The Eastern Han Dynasty (25–220 AD), a period of Chinese history.
  • 洛阳 - Luoyang, a major city in ancient China, particularly significant during the Han Dynasty.
  • 公卿 - High-ranking officials in the imperial court, often used to refer to the elite class.
  • 正始 - A period of the Wei Dynasty (240–249 AD), named after the era name of Emperor Cao Rui.
  • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Han Dynasty, located in present-day Xi'an.
  • 霸城 - A place near Chang'an, often associated with historical and mythological significance.
  • 铜人 - Bronze figures, often used in temples or as symbols of power and authority.
  • 汉阴生 - Legendary figure known for his miraculous abilities and mysterious appearances.
  • 渭桥 - A bridge over the Wei River in Chang'an, a significant location in ancient Chinese history.
  • 长安中谣言 - Folk sayings or rumors circulating in Chang'an, often reflecting local beliefs and superstitions.

Proper Nouns

  • 蓟子训 - Jì Zǐxùn, a legendary figure in Chinese folklore.
  • 东汉 - The Eastern Han Dynasty (25–220 AD).
  • 洛阳 - Luoyang, a major city in ancient China.
  • 公卿 - High-ranking officials in the imperial court.
  • 正始 - A period of the Wei Dynasty (240–249 AD).
  • 长安 - Chang'an, the capital of the Han Dynasty.
  • 霸城 - A place near Chang'an.
  • 铜人 - Bronze figures.
  • 汉阴生 - Hán Yīnshēng, another legendary figure.
  • 渭桥 - A bridge over the Wei River in Chang'an.

[9]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 数死而复生 - Literal meaning 'dying repeatedly and being revived'; could be interpreted as a metaphor for survival or resurrection.
  • 云复雨 - Literal meaning 'clouds and rain'; describes weather conditions.
  • 水五日必止 - Literal meaning 'the water will stop in five days'; indicates a prediction.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 谷城乡 - Likely a place name, possibly referring to a specific region or village in Chinese history.
  • 平常生 - A proper name, possibly a historical or legendary figure known for his extraordinary experiences.
  • 缺门山 - A place name, possibly a mountain or hill with cultural or historical significance.
  • 华阴 - A place name, likely referring to Huayin, a city in Shaanxi Province, China.
  • 左慈 - A proper name, likely referring to Zuo Ci, a historical figure known for his spiritual abilities.
  • 字符放 - A proper name, possibly a title or name given to Zuo Ci.
  • 庐江 - A place name, likely referring to Lujiang, a region in Anhui Province, China.
  • 吴松江鲈鱼 - Refers to a specific type of fish, the Wusongjiang (Wu River) perch, known for its flavor.
  • 锦肆 - Means 'silk shop,' referring to a place where silk was sold.
  • 端 - A unit of measurement for cloth, likely referring to a specific quantity of silk.

Proper Nouns

  • 谷城乡平常生 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical or legendary figure.
  • 缺门山 - A place name, possibly a mountain or hill with cultural or historical significance.
  • 华阴 - A place name, likely referring to Huayin, a city in Shaanxi Province, China.
  • 左慈 - A proper name, likely referring to Zuo Ci, a historical figure known for his spiritual abilities.
  • 字符放 - A proper name, possibly a title or name given to Zuo Ci.
  • 庐江 - A place name, likely referring to Lujiang, a region in Anhui Province, China.
  • 吴松江鲈鱼 - Refers to a specific type of fish, the Wusongjiang (Wu River) perch, known for its flavor.
  • 锦肆 - Means 'silk shop,' referring to a place where silk was sold.
  • 端 - A unit of measurement for cloth, likely referring to a specific quantity of silk.

[10]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 後公出近郊 - Literally means 'after the official went out to the outskirts,' indicating a departure from the city.
  • 士人從者百數 - 'Hundreds of scholars followed,' where '百數' is a hyperbolic expression meaning 'many' or 'a large number.'
  • 放乃赉酒一罂，脯一片 - 'Fang then brought a jar of wine and a piece of dried meat,' where '赉' means 'to bring' or 'to present.'
  • 手自倾罂，行酒百官 - 'He personally poured the wine for all the officials,' where '手自' emphasizes personal involvement.
  • 百官莫不醉饱 - 'All the officials were drunk and full,' where '莫不' means 'all without exception.'
  • 公怪，使寻其故 - 'The official was surprised and sent someone to find out the reason,' where '怪' means 'to be surprised' or 'to be puzzled.'
  • 行视沽酒家 - 'He went to the wine shop,' where '沽酒家' specifically refers to a place selling wine.
  • 昨悉亡其酒脯矣 - 'All the wine and dried meat had disappeared,' where '悉亡' means 'all vanished.'
  • 公怒，阴欲杀放 - 'The official was angry and secretly wanted to kill Fang,' where '阴' means 'secretly' or 'in secret.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '宅孝先，从左元放受九丹液仙经' is a compound structure where '宅孝先' describes Ge Xuan's residence, and '从左元放受九丹液仙经' describes his learning.
  • The sentence '与客对食，言及变化之事' introduces the setting and the topic of conversation.
  • The sentence '事毕，先生作一事特戏者' introduces the magical performance.
  • The sentence '君得无即欲有所见乎' sets up the expectation of a magical display.
  • The sentence '乃嗽口中饭，尽变大蜂数百，皆集客身，亦不螫人' describes the transformation of food into bees and their behavior.
  • The sentence '玄乃张口，蜂皆飞入，玄嚼食之，是故饭也' describes the reverse transformation.
  • The sentence '又指虾蟆及诸行虫燕雀之属，使舞，应节如人' describes the control over animals.
  • The sentence '冬为客设生瓜枣，夏致冰雪' describes the ability to manipulate temperature.
  • The sentence '又以数十钱使人散投井中，玄以一器于井上呼之，钱一一飞从井出' describes the control over objects.
  • The sentence '为客设酒，无人传杯，杯自至前，如或不尽，杯不去也' describes the ability to make the cup move.
  • The sentence '尝与吴主坐楼上，见作请雨土人' introduces the historical context.
  • The sentence '百姓思雨，宁可得乎' sets up the expectation of rain.
  • The sentence '雨易得耳' confirms the ease of obtaining rain.
  • The sentence '乃书符着社中，顷刻间，天地晦冥，大雨流淹' describes the immediate effect of the talisman.
  • The sentence '水中有鱼乎' sets up the expectation of fish.
  • The sentence '玄复书符掷水中，须臾，有大鱼数百头' describes the appearance of fish.
  • The sentence '使人治之' describes the preparation of the fish.

  • 侯为劾之，得大蛇数丈，死于门外，妇因以安 - This phrase is a sequence of actions that describes the Marquis exposing the spirit, capturing a large snake, and the woman recovering.
  • 又有大树，树有精，人止其下者死，鸟过之亦坠 - This phrase is a complex sentence that describes another large tree with a spirit, causing death and destruction.
  • 侯劾之，树盛夏枯落，有大蛇，长七八丈，悬死树间 - This phrase is a sequence of actions that describes the Marquis exposing the tree spirit, the tree withering and falling, and a large snake hanging dead in the tree.
  • 章帝闻之，征问 - This phrase is a simple sentence that describes Emperor Zhang hearing about the events and summoning the Marquis to inquire.
  • 对曰，有之 - This phrase is a simple response that confirms the events.
  • 殿下有怪，夜半后，常有数人，绛衣，披发，持火相随 - This phrase is a complex sentence that describes strange occurrences in the palace, including several people with red clothing, loose hair, and carrying torches.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 漢永平中 - A time expression meaning 'During the Yongping era of the Han dynasty.' 永平 is an era name (年號) of Emperor Ming of Han.
  • 會稽鐘離意，字子阿 - A standard classical biographical formula: 'Huiji Zhongli Yi, style name Zi A.'
  • 為魯相 - 'Served as the magistrate of Lu.' 魯相 is the title of the magistrate of the state of Lu.
  • 出私錢萬三千文 - 'Spent his own money, thirteen thousand coins.' In classical Chinese, 文 is the unit of coinage.
  • 付戶曹孔訴 - 'Gave to Kong Su, the head of the household affairs department.' 戶曹 refers to the official in charge of household and financial matters.
  • 修夫子車 - 'To repair the carriage of the Master.' 夫子 is a respectful term for Confucius.
  • 身入廟 - 'He personally entered the temple.' This emphasizes his personal involvement in the ritual.
  • 拭幾席劍履 - 'Wiped the table, mat, sword, and shoes.' These are ritual items in the Confucian temple.
  • 男子張伯除堂下草 - 'A man named Zhang Bo was weeding the courtyard.' 男子 here refers to a male commoner.
  • 土中得玉璧七枚 - 'Found seven jade discs in the soil.' 玉璧 is a type of ancient ritual jade object.
  • 伯懷其一 - 'Zhang Bo kept one for himself.' 懷 can mean 'to hide' or 'to keep in one’s possession.'
  • 以六枚白意 - 'He presented six to Yi.' 白 means to report or present to a superior.
  • 意令主簿安置幾前 - 'Yi ordered the clerk to place them before the table.' 主簿 is the official in charge of administrative records.
  • 孔子教授堂下床首有懸瓮 - 'In the hall where Confucius taught, at the head of the bed, there was a suspended jar.' This may refer to a ceremonial object in the temple.
  • 意召孔訴問 - 'Yi summoned Kong Su to ask.' This shows Yi’s authority and initiative.
  • 此何瓮也 - 'What is this jar?' A direct question.
  • 夫子瓮也 - 'It is the jar of the Master.' 夫子 again refers to Confucius.
  • 背有丹書，人莫敢發也 - 'There is a red script on the back; no one dares to open it.' 丹書 is a red-inscribed text, likely a sacred or magical writing.
  • 夫子，聖人。所以遺瓮，欲以懸示後賢 - 'The Master was a sage. He left this jar to hang as a sign for future scholars.' This is a philosophical or symbolic interpretation.
  • 中得素書 - 'Inside was found a white (or plain) book.' 素書 may refer to a scroll or a manuscript.
  • 文曰 - 'The text said' or 'It read.'
  • 後世修吾書，董仲舒 - 'For future generations to study my writings, Dong Zhongshu.' 董仲舒 is a famous Han dynasty Confucian scholar.
  • 護吾車拭吾履，發吾笥，會稽鐘離意 - 'To protect my carriage, wipe my shoes, and open my chest — Huiji Zhongli Yi.' This is a record of Yi’s actions.
  • 璧有七，張伯藏其一 - 'There were seven jade discs; Zhang Bo kept one.' This is a direct statement of fact.
  • 意即召問 - 'Yi immediately summoned him to question.' This shows Yi’s decisive action.
  • 伯叩頭出之 - 'Zhang Bo bowed and produced it.' 叩頭 is the act of bowing deeply in submission.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 漢永平中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Ming of Han (r. 57–75 CE), known for his promotion of Buddhism and Confucianism.
  • 會稽 - A region in present-day Zhejiang Province, historically an important area in the eastern part of China.
  • 魯相 - The magistrate of the state of Lu, one of the most important Confucian states in ancient China, as Confucius was born there.
  • 夫子 - A respectful term for Confucius, used in the context of the Confucian temple.
  • 玉璧 - A type of ancient ritual jade object used in religious and ceremonial contexts. It was often associated with heaven and the emperor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 须臾 - Classical term meaning 'a short while' or 'in a moment.'
  • 昔驺出 - '昔' means 'former' or 'past,' and '驺' refers to a servant or attendant. '昔驺出' indicates that a servant from the past appeared.
  • 引班如向法而进 - '引' means 'to lead' or 'to guide,' '班' is the name of the person, '向法' means 'according to the law' or 'following the proper procedure,' and '进' means 'to advance' or 'to proceed.' This phrase describes the servant leading 班 according to the proper procedure.
  • 因致书焉 - '因' means 'therefore' or 'thus,' '致' means 'to deliver' or 'to present,' and '书' means 'letter' or 'message.' This phrase indicates that a letter was delivered as a result.
  • 再报班，语讫，如厕 - '再报班' means 'to report to 班 again,' '语讫' means 'after finishing speaking,' and '如厕' means 'to go to the toilet.' This phrase describes the official reporting to 班 again after finishing speaking and then going to the toilet.
  • 忽见其父着械徒 - '忽' means 'suddenly,' '见' means 'to see,' '其父' means 'his father,' '着' means 'wearing,' '械' means 'chains' or 'fetters,' and '徒' means 'prisoner.' This phrase describes 班 suddenly seeing his father wearing chains and being a prisoner.
  • 作此辈数百人 - '作' means 'to be' or 'to appear,' '此辈' means 'these people,' and '数百人' means 'hundreds of people.' This phrase describes hundreds of people like his father.
  • 班进拜流涕问 - '进拜' means 'to approach and bow,' '流涕' means 'to weep,' and '问' means 'to ask.' This phrase describes 班 approaching, bowing, and weeping while asking.
  • 大人何因及此 - '大人' is a respectful term for a father or elder, '何因' means 'why' or 'what reason,' and '及此' means 'to come to this.' This phrase asks why the father has come to this situation.
  • 吾死不幸，见遣三年，今已二年矣 - '吾' means 'I,' '死不幸' means 'unfortunately died,' '见遣' means 'was sent,' '三年' means 'three years,' and '今已二年矣' means 'now it has been two years.'   • 乞免此役 - '乞' means 'to beg,' '免' means 'to exempt,' and '此役' means 'this labor.' This phrase describes the father begging to be exempted from this labor.

  • 生死异路，不可相近 - '生死' means 'life and death,' '异路' means 'different paths,' and '不可相近' means 'cannot be close.' This phrase describes the idea that life and death are different paths and cannot be close.
  • 身无所惜 - '身' means 'body' or 'life,' '无所惜' means 'nothing to be spared.' This phrase describes the official saying that his life is nothing to be spared.

  • 昔辞旷拙 - '昔辞' means 'former words,' '旷拙' means 'inadequate' or 'poorly expressed.' This phrase describes 班's former words being inadequate.
  • 及还家，儿死亡至尽 - '及还家' means 'when returning home,' '儿死亡' means 'children died,' and '至尽' means 'all.' This phrase describes 班's children all dying when he returned home.
  • 今恐祸故未已，辄来启白，幸蒙哀救 - '今恐祸故未已' means 'now fearing that the disaster has not yet ended,' '辄来启白' means 'came to inform,' '幸蒙哀救' means 'humbly request pity and rescue.' This phrase describes 班 fearing that the disaster has not yet ended and coming to inform, humbly requesting pity and rescue.
  • 府君拊掌大笑曰 - '府君拊掌大笑曰' means 'the official clapped his hands and laughed, saying.' This phrase describes the official clapping his hands and laughing, saying.

  • 即敕外召班父 - '即敕外召班父' means 'immediately ordered to summon 班's father.' This phrase describes the official immediately ordering to summon 班's father.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 俄见一人，年三十许，颜色如画，侍卫烦多，相对欣然，敕行酒，笑云：“仆有小女，颇聪明，欲以给君箕帚。” - This long sequence must be read as a single narrative of a supernatural encounter and proposal.
  • 此人知神，不敢拒逆。便敕：备办会就郎中婚。承白：已办。遂以丝布单衣，及纱袷绢裙，纱衫裈履屐，皆精好。又给十小吏，青衣数十人。妇年可十八九，姿容婉媚，便成。 - This sequence describes the preparation and completion of the marriage.
  • 三日，经大会客拜阁，四日，云：“礼既有限，发遣去。”妇以金瓯麝香囊与婿别，涕泣而分。又与钱十万，药方三卷，云：“可以施功布德。”复云：“十年当相迎。” - This sequence describes the wedding ceremony, the farewell, and the gifts.
  • 此人归家，遂不肯别婚，辞亲出家作道人。所得三卷方：一卷脉经，一卷汤方，一卷丸方。周行救疗，皆致神验。后母老，兄丧，因还婚宦。 - This sequence describes the man's life after the encounter, including his decision to become a monk, his travels, and his eventual return to normal life.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卿许活吾，当卒恩否？ - A direct question asking if the promise of life will be fulfilled. '卿' is a respectful term for 'you,' and '卒恩' refers to the fulfillment of a favor.
  • 大老子业已许卿，当复相欺耶！ - A statement emphasizing that a promise has already been made and cannot be broken. '大老子' is a colloquial term for 'elder' or 'father,' used here to convey a sense of authority and trust.
  • 信有水在上被之下，下被之上，不浸，如露之在荷 - A description of water that appears to be suspended between two layers of bedding, like dew on a lotus leaf. '信有' means 'indeed there is,' and '不浸' indicates that the water does not seep through.
  • 量之，得三升七合 - A precise measurement of the water, indicating a specific quantity. '三升七合' is a traditional Chinese unit of measurement.
  • 凡其所道当取者，皆死亡 - A statement that all those mentioned by name have died. '凡其所道' means 'all those mentioned,' and '当取者' refers to the individuals who were supposed to be taken.
  • 唯王文英，半年后乃亡 - An exception to the previous statement, noting that Wang Wenyi died six months later. '唯' means 'only' or 'just,' and '半年后乃亡' indicates the specific timing of his death.
  • 所道与赤笔人，皆经疾病及兵乱，皆亦无恙 - A statement that all those given red pens have survived despite illness and war. '所道' means 'those mentioned,' and '赤笔人' refers to the individuals given red pens. '皆经疾病及兵乱' indicates that they have experienced illness and war, while '皆亦无恙' means they have remained unharmed.
  • 初，有妖书云 - An introduction to a mythical or supernatural text. '妖书' refers to a book containing supernatural or magical content.
  • 上帝以三将军赵公明、钟士季各督数鬼下取人 - A statement about divine beings sending generals to take people. '上帝' means 'Heavenly Deity' or 'God,' and '三将军' refers to three generals. '赵公明' and '钟士季' are the names of the generals, and '督数鬼下取人' means they are leading spirits to take people.
  • 莫知所在 - A statement indicating that the location of the generals is unknown. '莫知' means 'no one knows,' and '所在' means 'where they are.'
  • 佑病差，见此书，与所道赵公明合焉 - A statement that after recovering from illness, the person sees a book that matches the description of Zhao Gongming. '病差' means 'recovered from illness,' and '与所道赵公明合焉' means 'matches the description of Zhao Gongming.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 佑 - The name of the main character, likely a historical figure or a fictional character in the story.
  • 赵公明 - A legendary deity in Chinese folklore, often associated with wealth and protection. He is one of the Four Heavenly Kings in Taoism.
  • 钟士季 - Another legendary figure, possibly a general or a deity, mentioned alongside Zhao Gongming.
  • 赤笔 - Red pens, which are often associated with blessings or protective symbols in Chinese culture.
  • 神以水灌我 - A supernatural event where a deity or spirit pours water over the person. This is a common motif in Chinese folklore and religious texts.
  • 三升七合 - A traditional Chinese unit of measurement, used to describe the amount of water.
  • 妖书 - A book containing supernatural or magical content, often associated with folklore and religious texts.
  • 上帝 - The Heavenly Deity or God, a concept found in various Chinese religious and philosophical traditions.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 以余浆溉灌 - 以 means 'with,' 余浆 means 'leftover water,' and 溉灌 means 'to irrigate.' This phrase describes using leftover water to irrigate the planted area.
  • 李君令我目愈 - 李君 refers to the plum tree as a deity, and 令我目愈 means 'healed my eye.' This phrase expresses gratitude to the plum tree for healing an eye ailment.
  • 众犬吠声 - 众犬 means 'many dogs,' and 吠声 means 'barking.' This idiom means that people follow the crowd or spread rumors without knowing the truth.
  • 盲者得视 - 盲者 means 'the blind,' and 得视 means 'gain sight.' This phrase describes the miraculous healing of blindness.
  • 翕赫 - 翕赫 means 'sudden and widespread fame' or 'great commotion.'
  • 下车骑常数千百 - 下车骑 means 'people getting off their carriages and riding horses,' and 常数千百 means 'often thousands.' This phrase describes a large number of people arriving by carriage and horse.
  • 酒肉滂沱 - 酒肉 means 'food and drink,' and 滂沱 means 'pouring down' or 'abundant.' This phrase describes a large amount of food and drink being provided.
  • 间一岁余 - 间 means 'after,' 一岁 means 'one year,' and 余 means 'more.' This phrase indicates a period of one year and more.
  • 此有何神 - 此 means 'this,' 何 means 'what,' and 神 means 'god' or 'spirit.' This phrase questions the presence of any divine power.
  • 乃我所种耳 - 乃 means 'it is,' 我 means 'I,' 所种 means 'planted,' and 耳 means 'only' or 'just.' This phrase clarifies that the growth was due to his own planting.
  • 因就斫之 - 因 means 'therefore,' 就 means 'go,' and 斫之 means 'cut it down.' This phrase describes the act of cutting down the tree.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南顿张助 - 南顿 is a place name, and 张助 is a person's name. This refers to Zhang Zhu from Nandun, who is the protagonist of the story.
  • 王莽居摄 - 王莽 is Wang Mang, a usurper who briefly ruled China during the Xin Dynasty. 居摄 means 'acting regent' or 'holding power temporarily.' This refers to Wang Mang's temporary rule.
  • 刘京上言 - 刘京 is Liu Jing, a person's name. 上言 means 'to report to the emperor.' This refers to Liu Jing reporting something to the emperor.
  • 齐郡临淄县亭长辛当 - 齐郡 is a place name, 临淄县 is another place name, and 亭长辛当 is a person's name. 亭长 was a local official responsible for maintaining order and collecting taxes. This refers to Xindang, the village head of Linzi County in Qi Province.
  • 数梦人谓曰 - 数梦 means 'often dreamed,' 人 means 'person,' and 谓曰 means 'said to.' This phrase describes frequent dreams where someone speaks.
  • 天使也 - 天使 means 'messenger of heaven' or 'god.' This phrase refers to a divine messenger.
  • 摄皇帝，当为真 - 摄皇帝 means 'acting emperor,' and 当为真 means 'should become the true emperor.' This phrase suggests that the acting emperor should become the legitimate ruler.
  • 此亭中当有新井出 - 此亭中 means 'in this village,' 当 means 'should,' and 新井出 means 'a new well will appear.' This phrase predicts the appearance of a new well in the village.
  • 入地百尺 - 入地 means 'go into the ground,' and 百尺 means 'a hundred feet.' This phrase describes a well that goes deep into the ground.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 妖怪者，蓋精氣之依物者也 - Classical definition of '妖怪' (monsters or spirits) as manifestations of spiritual energy attached to physical objects.
  • 氣亂於中，物變於外 - Metaphorical expression describing the internal chaos of spiritual energy and its external manifestation in physical changes.
  • 形神气质，表里之用也 - Classical phrase meaning 'the form, spirit, and essence are the inner and outer workings,' emphasizing the interplay between internal and external states.
  • 本于五行，通于五事 - Refers to the fundamental principles of the Five Elements (五行) and their connection to the Five Constant Virtues (五事), a key concept in Chinese cosmology and ethics.
  • 虽消息升降，化动万端 - Classical expression describing the constant flux and transformation of the universe, with '消息' meaning 'rise and fall' and '化动万端' meaning 'transformations in myriad forms.'
  • 休咎之征，皆可得域而论矣 - Formal statement meaning 'all signs of auspiciousness or misfortune can be categorized and discussed,' emphasizing the systematic study of omens.
  • 山默然自移，天下兵乱，社稷亡也 - Classical prophecy from the *Book of Changes* (易传) linking the silent movement of mountains to impending national turmoil and the fall of the state.
  • 不极之异 - Phrase meaning 'unusual events that defy explanation,' used to describe extraordinary phenomena like the movement of mountains.
  • 易世变号 - Formal expression meaning 'a change of dynasty or era,' often associated with catastrophic events.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 夏桀之时厉山亡 - Refers to the legendary collapse of Mount Lishan during the reign of Jie of Xia, a tyrant in Chinese history.
  • 秦始皇之时三山亡 - Refers to the disappearance of the Three Mountains during the reign of Qin Shi Huang, a significant event in Chinese mythology.
  • 周显王三十二年宋大邱社亡 - Refers to the loss of a significant shrine in the state of Song during the reign of King Xian of Zhou, a historical event tied to omens and divination.
  • 汉昭帝之末，陈留昌邑社亡 - Refers to the disappearance of a shrine in Changyi, Tengzhou, during the reign of Emperor Zhao of Han, another historical event linked to divine warnings.
  • 会稽山阴琅邪中有怪山 - Refers to a mysterious mountain in Shanxi, Shanxi, that appeared out of nowhere, a legendary event tied to supernatural phenomena.
  • 东武县山，亦一夕自亡去 - Refers to the disappearance of a mountain in Dongwu County, another legendary event tied to supernatural phenomena.
  • 交州脆州山移至青州 - Refers to the movement of a mountain from Jiaozhou to Qingzhou, another extraordinary event tied to omens.
  • 尚书金縢 - Refers to the *Book of Documents* (尚书), a canonical text in Chinese literature, which contains the passage about the movement of mountains as a sign of impending change.

Proper Nouns

  • 右角長三寸，左角長二寸 - Specifies the length of the right horn as three inches and the left horn as two inches.
  • 皆大二寸 - Indicates that both horns are two inches wide.
  • 劉向以為馬不當生角 - Duan Xia, a historian, believed that a horse should not grow horns.
  • 猶吳不當舉兵向上也 - Suggests that Wu should not have raised arms against the central authority.
  • 吳將反之變云 - Indicates a prediction or omen of rebellion by Wu.
  • 京房易傳曰 - Quotation from Jing Fang's commentary on the *I Ching*.
  • 臣易上，政不順 - Suggests that officials are replacing superiors, leading to political disorder.
  • 厥妖馬生角 - Refers to the supernatural phenomenon of a horse growing horns.
  • 茲謂賢士不足 - Implies that there is a shortage of virtuous officials.
  • 天子親伐，馬生角 - Suggests that if the emperor personally leads a campaign, it could lead to unusual phenomena like a horse growing horns.
  • 文帝后元五年六月 - Refers to the sixth month of the fifth year of Emperor Wen's posthumous reign.
  • 齊雍城门外有狗生角 - Literally 'in Qí, outside the city gate of Yōng, there was a dog that grew horns.'
  • 執政失下，將害之 - Suggests that officials are failing to govern properly, leading to harm.
  • 漢景帝元年九月 - Refers to the ninth month of the first year of Emperor Jing's reign.
  • 膠東下密人，年七十余，生角 - Literally 'in Jiadong, Xia Mi, a person over seventy years old, grew horns.'
  • 冢宰专政 - Refers to the chief minister exercising excessive power.
  • 漢景帝三年 - Refers to the third year of Emperor Jing's reign.
  • 邯鄲有狗与彘交 - Literally 'in Handan, there was a dog mating with a pig.'
  • 趙王悖亂 - Indicates that the King of Zhao was behaving in a rebellious manner.
  • 遂与六国反 - Suggests that he joined with six other states in rebellion.
  • 逆言失听 - Suggests that advice was ignored or misunderstood.
  • 交于异类 - Refers to mating with different species, symbolizing unnatural behavior.
  • 以生害也 - Implies that such behavior leads to harm.
  • 兹谓反德，国有兵革 - Suggests that such behavior indicates a reversal of virtue and leads to internal strife.

  • 堕泗水中死者数千 - Literal description of thousands of crows dying in the Si River, symbolizing significant loss or disaster.
  • 劉向以為近白黑祥也 - Reference to Liu Xiang's interpretation of the event as a sign of good or bad fortune, where '白黑祥' refers to auspicious or ominous signs.
  • 烏群斗者，師戰之象也 - Metaphorical interpretation of the crow fight as a symbol of military conflict.
  • 白颈者小，明小者敗也 - Symbolic interpretation where the smaller white-necked crows represent the weaker side in a conflict.
  • 墮於水者，將死水地 - Symbolic interpretation where the crows falling into the water predict a watery death.
  • 燕王旦之謀反也，又有一烏，一鵲，鬥於燕宮中池上，烏墮池死 - Parallel event involving a crow and a magpie fighting in the palace of Yan, with the crow falling into the pool and dying.
  • 五行志以為楚、燕皆骨肉，藩臣驕恣，而謀不義，俱有烏鵲鬥死之祥 - Reference to the *Wuxing Zhi* (Record of the Five Elements), which interprets both events as omens of rebellion by close relatives of the emperor.
  • 行同而占合，此天人之明表也 - Philosophical statement suggesting that the events are clear signs of the interaction between heaven and humanity.
  • 燕陰謀未發，獨王自殺於宮，故一烏而水色者死 - Description of the Yan plot not being discovered, leading to the king's suicide, and the crow falling into the water and dying.
  • 楚炕陽舉兵，軍師大敗於野，故烏眾而金色者死 - Description of the Chu forces raising an army, suffering a major defeat, and the black crows dying.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昭帝元鳳三年正月 - Refers to the first month of the third year of the Yuanshi era during Emperor Zhao's reign.
  • 泰山芜莱山南汹汹有数千人声 - Describes a loud noise resembling thousands of people coming from the south of Mount Wulai on Mount Tai.
  • 有大石自立 - Refers to a large stone standing up by itself.
  • 高丈五尺，大四十八围 - Describes the height and circumference of the stone.
  • 入地深八尺，三石为足 - Indicates the depth the stone is embedded in the ground and mentions three stones as its base.
  • 石立后，有白乌数千集其旁 - Describes thousands of white crows gathering around the stone after it stands up.
  • 宣帝中兴之瑞也 - Indicates that this event is a sign of the revival under Emperor Xuan.
  • 昭帝时上林苑中，大柳树断仆地 - Refers to a large willow tree breaking and falling to the ground in the Shanglin Park during Emperor Zhao's reign.
  • 一朝起立，生枝叶 - Describes the tree standing up again and growing branches and leaves.
  • 有虫食其叶，成文字，曰：“公孙病已立。” - Indicates that insects ate the leaves forming the words 'Gongsun Bingyi has been established.'
  • 昭帝时昌邑王贺见大白狗，冠“方山冠”而无尾 - Describes the sight of a large white dog wearing a 'Fangshan hat' without a tail seen by King Changyi during Emperor Zhao's reign.
  • 至熹平中，省内冠狗带绶以为笑乐 - Refers to the custom of wearing dogs with sashes inside the palace for amusement during the Xiping period.
  • 有一狗突出，走入司空府门 - Describes a dog suddenly running out and entering the door of the Siku official's residence.
  • 或见之者，莫不惊怪 - Indicates that those who saw it were all surprised.
  • 京房易传曰：“君不正，臣欲篡，厥妖狗冠出朝门。” - Quotes from Jing Fang's *Yi* transmission, which states that if the ruler is not upright, ministers may attempt to usurp power, and the sign of this is a dog wearing a hat appearing at the palace gate.
  • 汉宣帝黄龙元年，未央殿辂軨中雌鸡化为雄 - Refers to the year Huanglong of Emperor Xuan, when a hen in the Luoling area of Weiyang Palace turned into a rooster.
  • 毛衣变化，而不鸣，不将，无距 - Describes the hen turning into a rooster but not crowing, not leading, and having no spurs.
  • 元帝初元元年，丞相府史家雌鸡伏子，渐化为雄 - Refers to the year Chuyuan of Emperor Yuan, when a hen in the household of a historian in the Chancellor's office gradually turned into a rooster.
  • 冠距鸣将 - Describes the hen turning into a rooster with spurs, crowing, and leading.
  • 至永光中有献雄鸡生角者 - Refers to the year Yongguang when someone presented a rooster with horns.
  • 五行志以为王氏之应 - Indicates that the Five Elements Records consider these events signs of the Wang family's rise.
  • 京房易传曰：“贤者居明夷之世，知时而伤或众在位，厥妖鸡生角。” - Quotes from Jing Fang's *Yi* transmission, which states that if a wise person lives in a dark age and feels sorrow over many people in power, the sign of this is a rooster with horns.
  • 又曰：“妇人专政，国不静，牝鸡雄鸣，主不荣。” - Further quotes from Jing Fang's *Yi* transmission, which states that if women rule, the country will not be peaceful, and if a hen crows like a rooster, the ruler will not be honored.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 共取一妇 - Means 'share one wife'; describes a situation where three men share one wife.
  • 致争讼 - Means 'lead to litigation'; describes the dispute that arose from the shared wife.
  • 廷尉范延寿断之曰 - 'The Grand Judge Fan Yan寿 adjudicated and said'; refers to the judgment made by the highest judicial official.
  • 此非人类 - Means 'this is not human'; used to describe the unusual situation of three men sharing one wife.
  • 当以禽兽从母不从父也 - Means 'it should be treated as an animal, following the mother rather than the father'; suggests a solution to the dispute.
  • 戮三男 - Means 'execute the three men'; proposed solution to the dispute.
  • 以儿还母 - Means 'return the child to the mother'; proposed solution to the dispute.
  • 事何必古 - Means 'why must things be done according to ancient practices?'; Emperor Xuan's response questioning the need to follow ancient customs.
  • 若此，则可谓当于理而厌人情也 - Means 'if this is the case, it can be said to be in accordance with reason but unsatisfactory to human emotions'; further elaboration on Emperor Xuan's response.
  • 延寿盖见人事而知用刑矣 - Means 'Fan Yan寿 saw the human situation and knew how to apply punishment'; commentary on Fan Yan寿's judgment.
  • 未知论人妖将来之验也 - Means 'he did not know the future implications of human anomalies'; suggests that Fan Yan寿 did not foresee the future consequences of his judgment.
  • 天雨草，而叶相樛结 - Means 'it rained grass, and the leaves intertwined'; describes a natural phenomenon during the Yongguang era.
  • 大如弹丸 - Means 'as large as a bullet'; describes the size of the grass that rained down.
  • 天雨草，状如永光时 - Means 'it rained grass, similar to the time of Yongguang'; describes a similar natural phenomenon during the Yuanshi era.
  • 君吝于禄，信衰，贤去 - Means 'the ruler is stingy with rewards, trust is declining, and the virtuous are leaving'; quote from Jing Fang's *Yijing* commentary.
  • 厥妖天雨草 - Means 'the anomaly is the rain of grass'; further explanation of the natural phenomenon.
  • 禁民私所自立社 - Means 'prohibit the people from privately establishing shrines'; describes a policy by Hao Shang.
  • 山阳橐茅乡社有大槐树 - Refers to a large locust tree in the shrine of Tuomao Village, Shanyang; describes a specific location.
  • 吏伐断之，其夜树复立故处 - Means 'the official cut down the tree, but that night it stood up again in its original place'; describes a supernatural event.
  • 凡枯断复起，皆废而复兴之象也 - Means 'any dead thing that rises again is a sign of abandonment and revival'; general statement about the significance of such events.
  • 是世祖之应耳 - Means 'it is the response of the founding ancestor'; concludes the passage with a reference to the founding ancestor.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 雨魚 - A rare natural phenomenon described as 'rain of fish,' indicating unusual or supernatural occurrences.
  • 長五寸以下 - 'Length of five inches or less,' a specific measurement used to describe the size of the fish.
  • 北海出大魚 - 'A great fish appeared in the Bohai Sea,' referring to a large fish emerging from the sea.
  • 長六丈，高一丈 - 'Six zhang long, one zhang high,' a classical way of expressing the dimensions of a large fish.
  • 四枚 - 'Four in number,' indicating the quantity of fish.
  • 皆死 - 'All died,' indicating the fate of the fish after appearing.
  • 邪人進，賢人疏 - From *Jing Fang's Commentary on the Yi Jing*, this means 'evil men advance, virtuous men are sidelined.'
  • 木生为人状 - 'Trees grow in human form,' referring to a tree or branch that takes on the appearance of a human.
  • 眉目須皆具 - 'Eyes, eyebrows, and beard all present,' describing the detailed features of the branch.
  • 亡发耳 - 'Lacking hair on the ears,' indicating a minor imperfection in the human-like appearance.
  • 材仆地生枝 - 'A log fell to the ground and grew branches,' describing the miraculous growth of a branch from a fallen tree.
  • 身青黄色，面白，头有髭发 - 'Body greenish-yellow, face white, head with beard,' describing the physical characteristics of the branch.
  • 稍长大，凡长六寸一分 - 'Gradually growing larger, ultimately measuring six inches and one part,' indicating the final size of the branch.
  • 王德衰，下人将起 - 'When the virtue of the king declines, the lower people will rise up,' a warning from *Jing Fang's Commentary* about the decline of royal virtue.
  • 大厩马生角 - 'A horse in the imperial stables grew horns,' a sign of unusual or ominous occurrence.
  • 围长各二寸 - 'Each horn had a circumference of two inches,' describing the size of the horns.
  • 天水平襄有燕生雀 - 'In Tianping, Pingxiang, a swallow gave birth to a sparrow,' describing an unusual animal birth.
  • 哺食至大，俱飞去 - 'Fed until it grew large, and both flew away,' describing the growth and departure of the sparrow.
  • 贼臣在国，厥咎燕生雀，诸侯销 - 'When a traitorous minister is in the state, the fault is the swallow giving birth to a sparrow; the feudal lords will be destroyed.' A warning from *Jing Fang's Commentary*.
  • 生非其类，子不嗣世 - 'When something is born not of its kind, the offspring will not inherit the world,' a moral judgment on unnatural phenomena.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han, a ruler of the Western Han dynasty.
  • 鸿嘉四年 - The fourth year of the Hongjia era of Emperor Cheng.
  • 永始元年 - The first year of the Yongshi era of Emperor Cheng.
  • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of Han, successor to Emperor Cheng.
  • 建平三年 - The third year of the Jianping era of Emperor Ai.
  • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han, a later Han ruler known for his corruption and the fall of the dynasty.
  • 熹平二年 - The second year of the Xiping era of Emperor Ling.
  • 京房易传 - *Jing Fang's Commentary on the Yi Jing* (易傳), a commentary on the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) attributed to Jing Fang, a scholar of the Western Han.
  • 王莽 - Wang Mang, a regent and later usurper who overthrew the Han dynasty and founded the Xin dynasty.
  • 大厩 - The imperial stables, a place where royal horses were kept.
  • 天水平襄 - A location in the Western Han period, likely in the region of present-day Henan or Shaanxi provinces.
  • 燕生雀 - A swallow giving birth to a sparrow, an unnatural phenomenon interpreted as a sign of political instability or moral decay.

Proper Nouns

  • 成帝 - Emperor Cheng of Han
  • 哀帝 - Emperor Ai of Han
  • 灵帝 - Emperor Ling of Han
  • 王莽 - Wang Mang
  • 信都 - Xindu, a county in Hebei province
  • 北海 - Bohai Sea, a major body of water in northern China
  • 东莱 - Donglai, a region in Shandong province
  • 平度 - Pingdu, a place in Shandong province
  • 天水平襄 - Tianping, Pingxiang, likely a location in the Han dynasty
  • 京房易传 - *Jing Fang's Commentary on the Yi Jing* (易傳)
  • 大司马 - Grand Marshal, a high-ranking official position in the Han dynasty
  • 大厩 - The imperial stables

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 随群饮食，五行志以为：马，国之武用。三足，不任用之象也 - Following the herd in eating and drinking, the *Wuxing Zhi* (Book of the Five Elements) interprets: 'Horses are the martial tools of the state. Three legs are an omen of uselessness.'
  • 弃正，作淫，厥妖本断自属 - Abandoning the proper, indulging in excess, the omen is that the root will be cut and belong to itself.
  • 妃后有颛，木仆，反立，断枯，复生 - The empress has dominance, the tree falls, stands again, is cut and withered, but regrows.
  • 阳变为阴，将亡；继嗣，自相生之象 - Yang transforming into yin, foretells death; the heir, a self-generating image.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元始元年 - The first year of the Yuanshi era during the reign of Emperor Ping of Han.
  • 朔方广牧 - A place name referring to a region in the northern frontier.
  • 棺殓 - The act of placing a body in a coffin.
  • 出在棺外 - Literally 'appeared outside the coffin,' an idiom describing a supernatural or miraculous event.
  • 太守谭以闻 - 'Governor Tan reported it,' where '以闻' means 'to report' or 'to inform.'
  • 至阴为阳 - A philosophical concept suggesting that extreme conditions can transform into their opposites.
  • 下人为上 - Another philosophical concept implying that the lowly can rise to become superior.
  • 厥妖人死复生 - 'Such a marvel, a person dying and coming back to life,' where '厥' is a demonstrative pronoun.
  • 长安有女子生儿 - 'In Chang'an, a woman gave birth to a child,' where '生儿' specifically means 'gave birth to a boy.'
  • 尻上有目 - 'With eyes on the buttocks.'
  • 长二寸所 - 'About two inches long.'
  • 京房易传 - 'The Commentary on the Yi (Book of Changes) by Jing Fang,' an ancient commentary on the Yi Jing.
  • 暌孤见豕负涂 - 'The separation of the lonely, seeing pigs carrying mud,' a phrase from the Yi Jing.
  • 厥妖人生两头 - 'Such a marvel, a person being born with two heads.'
  • 下相攘 - 'The lower (people) are taking from each other.'
  • 善妖，亦同人 - 'Good omens, also like people.'
  • 若六畜 - 'Like livestock.'
  • 首目在下 - 'With heads and eyes below.'
  • 兹谓亡上 - 'This is called losing the superior.'
  • 政将变更 - 'The government will undergo changes.'
  • 厥妖之作 - 'Such marvels are created.'
  • 以谴失正 - 'To punish the loss of correctness.'
  • 各象其类 - 'Each represents its own kind.'
  • 两颈，下不一也 - 'Two necks, the lower is not unified.'
  • 手多，所任邪也 - 'Many hands, the one appointed is corrupt.'
  • 足少，下不胜任，或不任下也 - 'Few feet, the lower cannot bear the burden, or does not bear the lower.'
  • 凡下体生于上，不敬也 - 'All lower parts growing above, it is disrespectful.'
  • 上体生于下，媟渎也 - 'Upper parts growing below, it is disrespectful.'
  • 生非其类，淫乱也 - 'Born not of its kind, it is licentious.'
  • 人生而大，上速成也 - 'Born large, the upper quickly completes.'
  • 生而能言，好虚也 - 'Born able to speak, it is fond of emptiness.'
  • 群妖推此类 - 'All marvels follow this pattern.'
  • 不改，乃成凶也 - 'If not changed, it becomes an omen of disaster.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 元和元年 - The first year of the Yuanhe era of Emperor Zhang of Han.
  • 代郡高柳 - A place name, referring to a region in present-day Shanxi Province.
  • 三足 - A three-legged creature, which is unusual and often considered an omen.
  • 大如鸡 - As large as a chicken.
  • 色赤 - Red in color.
  • 头有角 - With horns on its head.
  • 长寸余 - About an inch long.
  • 汉桓帝即位 - Emperor Huan of Han ascended the throne.
  • 大蛇见德阳殿上 - A large snake appeared on the De Yang Palace.
  • 洛阳市令淳于翼 - The magistrate of Luoyang named Chunyu Yi.
  • 蛇有鳞 - Snakes have scales.
  • 甲兵之象也 - A symbol of military force.
  • 见于省中 - Appearing in the imperial palace.
  • 将有椒房大臣受甲兵之象也 - It indicates that a powerful minister will be involved in military matters.
  • 乃弃官遁去 - He then resigned and fled.
  • 到延熹二年 - By the second year of the Yuanxi era.
  • 诛大将军梁冀 - Executed the Grand General Liang Ji.
  • 捕治家属 - Arrested and punished his family.
  • 扬兵京师也 - Displayed military power in the capital.
  • 汉桓帝建和三年秋七月 - The seventh month of autumn in the third year of the Jianhe era of Emperor Huan of Han.
  • 北地廉雨肉 - In the north, meat rained down.
  • 似羊肋 - Resembling sheep ribs.
  • 或大如手 - Some as large as a hand.
  • 是时梁太后摄政 - At that time, Empress Dowager Liang was regent.
  • 梁冀专权 - Liang Ji held absolute power.
  • 擅杀 - Committed arbitrary killings.
  • 诛太尉李固、杜乔 - Executed the Grand Commandant Li Gu and Du Qiao.
  • 天下冤之 - The whole country mourned them.
  • 其后 - Afterward.
  • 梁氏诛灭 - The Liang family was exterminated.
  • 汉桓帝元嘉中 - During the Yuangjia era of Emperor Huan of Han.
  • 京都妇女作“愁眉”“啼妆”“堕马髻”“折腰步”“龋齿笑。” - Women in the capital made up their eyebrows in a sorrowful style, wore tearful makeup, styled their hair in a drooping manner, walked with bent waists, and laughed with clenched teeth.

  • 啼妆 - Tearful makeup, lightly applied under the eyes.
  • 堕马髻 - Hair styled to one side, resembling a horse falling.
  • 折腰步 - Walking with a bent waist.
  • 龋齿笑 - Laughing with clenched teeth.
  • 始自大将军梁冀妻孙寿所为 - Started by the wife of Grand General Liang Ji, Sun Shou.
  • 京都翕然 - The capital followed suit.
  • 诸夏效之 - All regions emulated this.
  • 天戒若曰 - Heaven seems to say.
  • 兵马将往收捕 - Soldiers will go to capture.
  • 妇女忧愁 - Women will be sad.
  • 踧眉啼哭 - Frown and cry.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 有牛生雞 - Literal description of an unusual event where a cow gave birth to a chicken.
  • 兩頭四足 - Literal description of a physical anomaly, indicating the creature had two heads and four feet.
  • 身為估服 - Dressing up as a merchant, indicating a role-playing activity.
  • 行至舍，問采女下酒食 - Walking to the inn and asking the ladies to serve wine and food, another part of the role-playing.
  • 因共飲食，以為戲樂 - Enjoying meals together, considering it a form of entertainment.
  • 是天子將欲失位，降在皂隸之謠也 - Interprets the unusual birth as a sign of the emperor's impending downfall and demotion to a lowly status.
  • 其後天下大亂 - Predicts widespread chaos following the unusual birth.
  • 赤厄三七 - A prophecy about a period of 210 years (three sevens) marked by calamity.
  • 三七者經二百一十載 - Specifies that the period of 210 years is the duration of the calamity.
  • 當有外戚之篡 - Predicts a usurpation by an external family member.
  • 丹眉之妖 - Refers to a red-haired or red-mustached figure, often associated with rebellion.
  • 篡盗短祚 - Describes a short-lived usurpation.
  • 極于三六 - Refers to a period of 18 years (three sixes).
  • 當有飛龍之秀 - Predicts the emergence of a remarkable figure, often associated with a hero or savior.
  • 興復祖宗 - Describes the restoration of the imperial lineage.
  • 又歷三七，當復有黄首之妖 - Predicts another period of 210 years marked by another calamity, this time associated with a figure with yellow hair or beard.
  • 天下大亂矣 - Predicts widespread chaos again.
  • 自高祖建業，至於平帝之末，二百一十年 - Refers to the period from the founding of the Han dynasty by Emperor Gaozu to the end of Emperor Ping's reign, spanning 210 years.
  • 王莽篡 - Describes the usurpation by Wang Mang.
  • 蓋因母后之親 - Suggests that the usurpation was due to the influence of the empress dowager.
  • 十八年而山东贼樊子都等起 - Describes a rebellion led by rebels named Fan Zidu, lasting 18 years.
  • 實丹其眉 - Indicates that the rebels had red hair or mustaches.
  • 故天下号曰“赤眉” - Explains why the rebels were called the 'Red Eyebrows.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 光武中興 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Guangwu of Han, marking the beginning of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
  • 三十六方 - Refers to the 36 regions or factions of the Yellow Turban Rebellion.
  • 道服 - Refers to Taoist clothing, which became associated with the movement.
  • 甲子年 - Refers to the year 184 CE, the start of the rebellion.
  • 未盈二百一十年 - Means "less than 210 years," referring to the time between Emperor Guangwu's reign and the start of the rebellion.
  • 建寧中 - Refers to the Jianning era, a period during the reign of Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 長服 - Refers to long robes, a fashion trend during this period.
  • 長裾 - Refers to long skirts, another fashion trend.
  • 天下未欲平也 - Means "the world is not yet at peace," indicating social unrest.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han, who restored the Han Dynasty after the fall of the Western Han.
  • 黃巾 - The Yellow Turbans, a Taoist sect that led the rebellion.
  • 靈帝 - Emperor Ling of Han, whose reign saw the rise of the Yellow Turban Rebellion.
  • 建寧 - The era name of Emperor Ling of Han, used during his reign.
  • 荊、揚 - Refers to the provinces of Jing and Yang, regions where the rebellion was particularly severe.
  • 甲子年 - The year 184 CE, when the Yellow Turban Rebellion began.
  • 三七之運 - Refers to the 210-year cycle (3 x 70) in Chinese numerology, symbolizing the end of an era.

  • 光武 - Emperor Guangwu of Han.
  • 秀 - Liu Xiu, the founder of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
  • 靈帝 - Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 建寧 - The era name of Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 邺 - The city of Ye, where the rebellion began.
  • 真定 - The city of Zhending, where the rebels gathered.
  • 荊 - The province of Jing.
  • 揚 - The province of Yang.

[82]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 靈帝建寧三年春 - Refers to the third year of the Jianning era during Emperor Ling's reign, a specific historical period.
  • 河内有婦食夫，河南有夫食婦 - Describes unusual and disturbing events where women ate their husbands and husbands ate their wives, respectively. These are literal translations but may require explanation in English to convey the gravity and symbolism.
  • 夫婦陰陽，二儀有情之深者也 - A philosophical statement about the deep emotional connection between husband and wife, likened to the yin and yang principles of the cosmos.
  • 今反相食，陰陽相侵，豈特日月之眚哉 - Suggests that these events are not just minor celestial disturbances but signs of deeper moral or cosmic imbalance.
  • 靈帝既沒，天下大亂 - Indicates that after Emperor Ling's death, widespread chaos ensued.
  • 君有妄誅之暴，臣有劫弑之逆 - Describes the violent actions of rulers and officials, including unjust killings and rebellious acts.
  • 故人妖為之先作 - Suggests that these unnatural events are precursors to greater calamities.
  • 恨不遭辛有、屠乘之論，以測其情也 - Expresses regret that the author did not live in a time when scholars like Xin You and Tu Cheng could provide deeper insights.
  • 靈帝熹平二年六月 - Refers to the second month of the Xiping era during Emperor Ling's reign.
  • 虎賁寺東壁中，有黃人，形容須眉良是 - Describes a rumor about a yellow-skinned person appearing in the wall of a temple.
  • 觀者數萬 - Indicates that thousands of people gathered to see this phenomenon.
  • 省內悉出，道路斷絕 - Describes the disruption caused by the large crowd.
  • 中平元年二月 - Refers to the first month of the Zhongping era during Emperor Ling's reign.
  • 張角兄弟起兵冀州 - Describes the rebellion led by Zhang Jiao and his brothers in Jizhou.
  • 三十六方，四面出和 - Describes the widespread nature of the rebellion.
  • 將帥星布，吏士外屬 - Describes the disorganization and defection of military and administrative personnel.
  • 因其疲餧牽而勝之 - Suggests that the rebels were defeated due to their own exhaustion and lack of resources.
  • 靈帝熹平三年 - Refers to the third year of the Xiping era during Emperor Ling's reign.
  • 右校別作中，有兩樗樹 - Describes two large elm trees in a specific location.
  • 宿昔暴長，長一丈餘，麤大一圍，作胡人狀 - Describes the rapid and unusual growth of one of the trees, which took on the appearance of a foreigner.

  • 其於洪範皆為木不曲直 - Refers to the *Book of Changes* (Yijing) and suggests that these events are signs of disorder in the natural world.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 靈帝光和元年 - A specific date in the reign of Emperor Ling of Han, marking the time of the event.
  • 南宮侍中寺 - Refers to a specific location in the imperial palace, likely a residence or office for the Attendant-in-Attendance.
  • 雌雞欲化為雄 - An idiom describing a hen chicken that appears to be transforming into a rooster, symbolizing unusual or unnatural phenomena.
  • 一身毛皆似雄，但頭冠尚未變 - Describes the hen's physical transformation, noting that while its feathers resemble those of a rooster, its head crest has not yet changed.
  • 靈帝光和二年 - Another specific date in the reign of Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 上西門外 - Refers to the western gate of the imperial city, indicating the location of the event.
  • 兩頭，異肩，共胸，俱前 - Describes the physical appearance of a two-headed child, emphasizing the abnormality.
  • 向以為不祥，墮地，棄之 - Indicates the belief that the child was an omen of bad luck, leading to its being abandoned.
  • 自是之後，朝廷霿亂，政在私門，上下無別，二頭之象 - A metaphorical statement suggesting that the two-headed child symbolized the disorder and lack of distinction between public and private life in the court.
  • 後董卓戮太后 - Refers to the historical event where Dong Zhuo killed Empress Dowager He.
  • 被以不孝之名，放廢天子，後復害之 - Describes the actions of Dong Zhuo, who was accused of filial impiety, deposed Emperor Ling, and later killed him.
  • 漢元以來，禍莫踰此 - Indicates that the events described were among the most significant calamities since the founding of the Han dynasty.
  • 光和四年 - Another specific date in the reign of Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 南宮中黃門寺 - Refers to another specific location in the imperial palace, likely a residence or office for the Yellow Gate Attendant.
  • 一男子，長九尺，服白衣 - Describes a tall man dressed in white, indicating a supernatural or unusual appearance.
  • 中黃門解步呵問 - Refers to the Yellow Gate Attendant named Xie Bu, who questioned the man.
  • 汝何等人 - A question asking the man to identify himself.
  • 我梁伯夏。後天使我為天子 - The man's response, claiming to be Liang Bexia and that he was appointed by the heavens to be emperor.
  • 步欲前收之，因忽不見 - Indicates that Xie Bu attempted to apprehend the man, but he disappeared suddenly.
  • 光和七年 - The final specific date in the reign of Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 陳留、濟陽、長垣、濟陰、東郡、冤句、離狐界中 - Refers to several regions in the empire, indicating the widespread nature of the phenomenon.
  • 路邊生草，悉作人狀，操持兵弩 - Describes grass growing along the roadside that appeared to be human figures holding weapons.
  • 牛馬龍蛇鳥獸之形，白黑各如其色，羽毛頭目足翅皆備 - Describes various animal forms appearing in the grass, with distinct colors and features.
  • 非但彷佛，像之尤純 - Indicates that the figures were not just vaguely similar to animals but were highly detailed and realistic.
  • 舊說曰：“近草妖也” - Refers to an ancient saying that such phenomena were signs of grass demons.
  • 是歲有黃巾賊起，漢遂微弱 - Indicates that the same year saw the rise of the Yellow Turban Rebellion, which weakened the Han dynasty.

  • 安吴民陈焦死，七日，复生，穿冢出乌程 - Describes an unusual event where a man named Chen Jiao died, came back to life after seven days, and emerged from a grave in Wu County.
  • 孙皓承废故之家得位之祥也 - Implies that the rise of Sun Hao was seen as a sign of good fortune for the fallen families.
  • 衣服之制，上长，下短，又积领五六，而裳居一二 - Describes a fashion trend where the upper part of the clothing was longer and the lower part was shorter, with multiple layers on top and fewer on bottom.
  • 盖上饶奢，下俭逼，上有余，下不足之象也 - Suggests that this fashion trend reflects social inequality, with the upper class being more extravagant and the lower class being more frugal.
  • 魏年有和，当有开石于西三千余里，系五马，文曰：‘大讨曹。’ - Describes a prophecy about a stone opening in the west, with five horses tied to it, inscribed with 'Greatly Destroy Cao.'
  • 始见于建安，形成于黄初，文备于太和，周围七寻，中高一仞，苍质素章 - Describes the stone's appearance and characteristics over time.
  • 龙、马、鳞、鹿、凤凰、仙人之象，粲然咸着 - Lists the various symbols and figures depicted on the stone.
  • 此一事者，魏、晋代兴之符也 - Suggests that this event is a sign of the rise of the Wei and Jin dynasties.
  • 至晋泰始三年，张掖太守焦胜上言 - Indicates the year and the official who reported the event.

  • 案其文有五马象 - Indicates that the stone has five horse figures.

  • 其一，有若马形而不成，其字有金，有中，有大司马，有王，有大吉，有正，有开寿 - Describes another horse figure and the inscriptions on it.

  • 妇人出两裆，加乎交领之上 - Describes women wearing two pieces of clothing over their collars, indicating a change in women's attire.

  • 为车乘者，苟贵轻细 - Refers to the preference for lightweight and delicate vehicles, indicating a trend in transportation.
  • 数变易其形 - Means that the design of these vehicles frequently changed, suggesting a lack of stability in fashion.
  • 白篾为纯 - Refers to the use of white bamboo strips as decorative elements, possibly alluding to mourning practices.
  • 古丧车之遗象 - Suggests that this practice is reminiscent of ancient funeral vehicles, hinting at a decline in societal norms.
  • 晋之祸征也 - Indicates that these trends are omens of disaster for the Jin dynasty.
  • 胡床，貊盘，翟之器也 - Refers to foreign furniture and utensils, indicating cultural influences from non-Han peoples.
  • 羌煮，貊炙，翟之食也 - Describes foreign foods, indicating dietary influences from non-Han peoples.
  • 自太始以来，中国尚之 - Means that since the Tai Shi era, these foreign elements have been favored in China.
  • 贵人，富室，必畜其器 - Indicates that wealthy and influential people would own these foreign items.
  • 吉享嘉宾，皆以为先 - Means that these items were used first in important social gatherings.
  • 戎翟侵中国之前兆也 - Suggests that the adoption of these foreign elements is a sign of impending invasion by non-Han peoples.
  • 太康四年 - Refers to the fourth year of the Tai Kang era (283 AD) of Emperor Wu of Jin.
  • 会稽郡蟛蚑及蟹，皆化为鼠 - Describes an unusual natural phenomenon where crabs and frogs turned into mice, indicating a supernatural event.
  • 为灾 - Means that this event caused widespread destruction, particularly by eating rice.
  • 有毛肉而无骨 - Describes the mice as having fur and flesh but no bones, suggesting a bizarre transformation.
  • 其行不能过田● - Means that the mice could not move beyond a certain point, possibly indicating a supernatural limitation.
  • 数日之后，则皆为牝 - Means that after a few days, all the mice became female, indicating a further supernatural change.
  • 太康五年正月，二龙见武库井中 - Describes a supernatural event where two dragons appeared in the well of the imperial armory.
  • 武库者，帝王威御之器，所宝藏也 - Refers to the imperial armory, a place where the emperor's weapons and treasures are stored.
  • 屋宇邃密，非龙所处 - Means that the armory is a deep and secure place, not suitable for dragons.
  • 是后七年，藩王相害 - Indicates that seven years later, there was conflict among the princes.
  • 二十八年，果有二胡，僭窃神器，皆字曰龙 - Describes a future event where two Xianbei leaders, named Long, usurped power, indicating a fulfillment of the earlier omen.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 初，作屐者：婦人圓頭，男子方頭 - Description of the initial design of shoes, where women's shoes had rounded tops and men's shoes had square tops.
  • 蓋作意欲別男女也 - The purpose behind this design was to distinguish between men and women.
  • 至太康中，婦人皆方頭屐，與男無異 - By the time of the Tai Kang period, women's shoes also had square tops, making no difference from men's.
  • 此賈后專妒之征也 - This was seen as a sign of the jealousy of Empress Jia Nanfeng.
  • 始自宮中，天下翕然化之也 - This style first started in the palace and quickly spread throughout the country.
  • 其末年，遂有懷、惠之事 - By the end of the dynasty, there were incidents involving Emperor Huai and Emperor Huizong.
  • 太康中，天下為“晉世寧”之舞 - During the Tai Kang period, people performed the 'Jin Shi Ning' dance.
  • 其舞，抑手以執杯盤，而反覆之 - In this dance, the hands were pressed down to hold cups and plates, which were then repeatedly flipped.
  • 歌曰：“晉世寧舞，杯盤反覆” - The song accompanying the dance said, 'The Jin Shi Ning dance, cups and plates are repeatedly flipped.'
  • 至危也 - This was a sign of danger.
  • 杯盤，酒器也，而名曰“晉世寧”者，言時人苟且飲食之間，而其智不可及遠，如器在手也 - Cups and plates are drinking vessels, but naming the dance 'Jin Shi Ning' suggests that people were living in a state of complacency, unable to think far ahead, like holding a vessel in their hands.
  • 太康中，天下以氈為絔頭，及絡帶褲口 - During the Tai Kang period, people used felt to make socks and to decorate the waistbands and cuffs of trousers.
  • 於是百姓咸相戲曰：“中國其必為胡所破也” - People joked among themselves, saying, 'China will surely be conquered by the barbarians.'
  • 夫氈，胡之所產者也，而天下以為絔頭，帶身，褲口，胡既三制之矣，能無敗乎 - Felt is produced by the barbarians, and if the Chinese use it for socks, waistbands, and cuffs, the barbarians have three ways to control them, how can they avoid being defeated?

  • 絔頭 - Socks or stockings.
  • 絡帶褲口 - Decorations on the waistbands and cuffs of trousers.

  • 永嘉五年十一月 - Refers to November of the fifth year of Emperor Huai of Jin's reign during the Yongjia period (311 AD).

  • 郭璞筮之，遇临之益 - Describes Guo Pu divining and encountering a hexagram indicating both danger and gain.
  • 此郡之东县，当有妖人欲称制者。寻亦自死矣 - A prediction that a malevolent person in one of the eastern counties would attempt to seize power but would soon die.
  • 永嘉六年正月 - Refers to January of the sixth year of Emperor Huai of Jin's reign during the Yongjia period (312 AD).
  • 无锡县欻有四枝茱萸树，相樛而生，状若连理 - Describes four branches of the *zhu yu* tree growing intertwined, resembling a *lian li* tree.
  • 先是，郭璞筮延陵蝘鼠，遇临之益，曰：“后当复有妖树生，若瑞而非，辛螫之木也。傥有此，东西数百里，必有作逆者。” - Describes Guo Pu's earlier prediction that a strange tree would grow, indicating potential rebellion.
  • 及此生木，其后吴兴徐馥作乱，杀太守袁琇 - Describes the subsequent rebellion by Xu Fu in Wuxing, resulting in the death of the governor Yuan Xiu.
