Idiomatic Expressions

  • 永嘉中 - The Yongjia period (307-313 AD), a time of political instability and turmoil.
  • 豕生人 - Literally 'pig giving birth to a person,' referring to a birth anomaly.
  • 两头而不活 - Means 'with two heads but not surviving,' describing the condition of the birth anomaly.
  • 周馥取而观之 - Means 'Zhou Fu took it to observe,' indicating the action of someone observing the anomaly.
  • 识者云 - Means 'those who know say,' introducing a statement by knowledgeable individuals.
  • 豕，北方畜 - Means 'pig, a northern livestock,' referring to the pig as a symbol of the northern tribes.
  • 胡狄象 - Means 'a symbol of Hu and Di,' where Hu and Di refer to northern nomadic tribes.
  • 两头者，无上也 - Means 'two heads mean no ruler,' suggesting a lack of leadership or authority.
  • 生而死，不遂也 - Means 'born and died, not succeeding,' indicating the failure of the anomaly to survive.
  • 天戒若曰 - Means 'heavenly warning as if saying,' introducing a divine message.
  • 易生专利之谋 - Means 'easy to generate profit-seeking schemes,' referring to short-sighted and greedy plans.
  • 将自致倾覆也 - Means 'will lead to self-destruction,' predicting the consequences of such schemes.
  • 士大夫竞服生笺单衣 - Means 'scholars and officials competed to wear freshly made single-layered clothes,' describing a fashion trend.
  • 无故服之 - Means 'wore them without reason,' indicating the unusual nature of the trend.
  • 殆有应乎 - Means 'probably there is a response,' suggesting a potential explanation or omen.
  • 怀、愍晏驾 - Refers to the abdication of Emperor Huai and Emperor Min, the last two emperors of the Western Jin Dynasty.
  • 魏武军中无故作白帢 - Means 'in the army of Cao Cao, they made white hats without reason,' referring to a historical event.
  • 缟素凶丧之征也 - Means 'a sign of mourning and misfortune,' indicating a bad omen.
  • 横缝其前以别后 - Means 'stitched horizontally at the front to distinguish the back,' describing the construction of the hat.
  • 传行之 - Means 'passed it on,' indicating the spread of the hat.
  • 至永嘉之间 - Means 'by the time of the Yongjia period,' setting the time frame.
  • 稍去其缝 - Means 'gradually removed the stitching,' describing a change in the hat's design.
  • 妇人束发 - Means 'women tied their hair,' describing a fashion trend among women.
  • 其缓弥甚 - Means 'its looseness increased more and more,' indicating a trend towards looser hairstyles.
  • 紒之坚不能自立 - Means 'the hair could not stand up on its own,' describing the effect of the loose hairstyle.
  • 发被于额，目出而已 - Means 'hair fell over the forehead, and only the eyes were visible,' describing the appearance of the loose hairstyle.
  • 无颜者，愧之言也 - Means 'No-Face means a word of shame,' explaining the meaning of the name 'No-Face Hat.'
  • 覆额者，惭之貌也 - Means 'covering the forehead means a look of shame,' explaining the appearance of the loose hairstyle.
  • 亡礼与义 - Means 'loss of ritual and righteousness,' suggesting a decline in moral values.
  • 放纵情性 - Means 'letting loose emotions and nature,' describing a lack of self-control.
  • 及其终极 - Means 'until its ultimate end,' indicating the final stage of the trend.
  • 至于大耻也 - Means 'until it became a great shame,' predicting the consequences of the trend.
  • 永嘉之乱 - The Yongjia Rebellion, a series of uprisings that led to the fall of the Western Jin Dynasty.
  • 四海分崩 - A phrase used to describe widespread chaos and division, often associated with the fall of a dynasty.
  • 下人悲难 - Describes the suffering of ordinary people during times of political turmoil.
  • 无颜以生焉 - A phrase indicating the deep shame and despair felt by people during the chaos.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 永嘉中 - The Yongjia period (307-313 AD) was marked by political instability and the rise of the Five Barbarians.
  • 周馥 - A high-ranking official during the Western Jin Dynasty, known for his role in the government.
  • 胡狄 - Refers to the northern nomadic tribes, particularly the Xiongnu, Xianbei, Jie, Di, and Qiang.
  • 怀、愍晏驾 - The abdication of Emperor Huai and Emperor Min, marking the end of the Western Jin Dynasty.
  • 魏武军中无故作白帢 - A historical event during the Three Kingdoms period, where Cao Cao's army made white hats, which later became a symbol of mourning.
  • 永嘉之乱 - The Yongjia Rebellion, a series of uprisings that led to the fall of the Western Jin Dynasty and the beginning of the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
  • 四海分崩 - A phrase used to describe widespread chaos and division, often associated with the fall of a dynasty.
  • 下人悲难 - Describes the suffering of ordinary people during times of political turmoil.
  • 无颜以生焉 - A phrase indicating the deep shame and despair felt by people during the chaos.

  • 无颜 - A term used to describe a state of shame and despair.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 晋愍帝建兴四年 - Refers to the fourth year of the Jianxing era during the reign of Emperor Min of Jin, a period marked by political turmoil and the fall of the Western Jin dynasty.
  • 元皇帝始为晋王四海宅心 - Indicates the establishment of the Eastern Jin dynasty, with Emperor Yuan as its first ruler.
  • 新蔡县吏任乔妻胡氏年二十五 - Refers to the wife of a county official named Ren Qiao from Xincai County, who was 25 years old.
  • 产二女，相向，腹心合，自腰以上，脐以下。各分 - Describes a rare birth where two girls were born facing each other, their upper bodies joined from the waist up, but separate below the navel.
  • 此盖天下未一之妖也 - Means 'this is a sign of the world not yet unified.'
  • 内史吕会上言 - Refers to the governor of the region, Lü Hui, who submitted a report.
  • 按瑞应图云 - Means 'according to the Book of Auspicious Signs.'
  • 异根同体，谓之连理 - Means 'different roots but the same body, called intertwined branches.'
  • 异亩同颍，谓之嘉禾 - Means 'different fields but the same grain, called auspicious rice.'
  • 草木之属，犹以为瑞 - Means 'even plants are considered auspicious.'
  • 今二人同心，天垂灵象 - Means 'now two people with one heart, a divine sign from heaven.'
  • 易云 - Refers to the Book of Changes.
  • 二人同心，其利断金 - Means 'two people with one heart, their strength can cut through metal.'
  • 休显见生于陈东之中 - Means 'the auspicious sign appears in the eastern part of Chen.'
  • 盖四海同心之瑞 - Means 'it is a sign of the four seas being united in heart.'
  • 不胜喜跃 - Means 'overjoyed.'
  • 谨画图上 - Means 'I respectfully draw a diagram and present it.'
  • 时有识者哂之 - Means 'at the time, those with knowledge laughed at it.'
  • 君子曰 - Means 'the virtuous say.'
  • 知之难也 - Means 'it is difficult to know.'
  • 以臧文仲之才，独祀爰居焉 - Means 'even with the talent of Zang Wenzhong, he alone worshipped the sea gull.'
  • 布在方册，千载不忘 - Means 'recorded in books, never forgotten for a thousand years.'
  • 故士不可以不学 - Means 'therefore, scholars cannot avoid learning.'
  • 古人有言 - Means 'ancient people said.'
  • 木无枝谓之瘣，人不学谓之瞽 - Means 'a tree without branches is called a cripple, a person without learning is called blind.'
  • 当其所蔽，盖阙如也 - Means 'when one is blinded, it is as if there is a gap.'
  • 可不勉乎 - Means 'how can one not strive?'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 太興中 - Specific time period during the reign of Emperor Yuan of Jin (318-322 AD).
  • 王敦鎮武昌 - Refers to Wang Dun's military governorship in Wuchang.
  • 武昌災 - Indicates a disaster in Wuchang.
  • 火起 - Describes the outbreak of fire.
  • 興眾救之 - Means to mobilize people to rescue the situation.
  • 救于此，而发于彼 - Describes the spread of the fire from one place to another.
  • 东西南北数十处俱应 - Indicates the widespread nature of the disaster.
  • 数日不绝 - Means the disaster lasted for several days.
  • 滥灾妄起，虽兴师不能救之 - Refers to a disaster that arises without reason and cannot be stopped even by mobilizing troops.
  • 臣而行君，亢阳失节 - Describes a situation where a minister acts like a ruler, indicating a loss of proper order.
  • 王敦陵上，有无君之心 - Refers to Wang Dun's disrespectful behavior towards the emperor, indicating his ambition to usurp power.
  • 太兴中兵士以绛囊缚紒 - Describes a custom where soldiers in the Taixing period wore red bags tied around their hair.
  • 紒在首，为干，君道也 - Explains that the hairband represents the 'Heavenly' principle, symbolizing the ruler.
  • 囊者，为坤，臣道也 - Explains that the bag represents the 'Earthly' principle, symbolizing the minister.
  • 今以朱囊缚紒 - Describes the current practice of tying a red bag around the hairband.
  • 臣道侵君之象也 - Indicates that this practice symbolizes the minister encroaching on the ruler's authority.
  • 为衣者上带短纔至于掖 - Describes a custom where the upper part of the belt only reaches the armpit.
  • 着帽者，又以带缚项 - Describes a custom where people tie the belt around their necks when wearing hats.
  • 下逼上，上无地也 - Indicates that the lower part of the belt is pressing against the upper part, leaving no room for the upper part.
  • 为裤者，直幅，无口，无杀，下大之象也 - Describes a custom where trousers are straight and have no waistband or hem, indicating a symbol of the lower part being too large.
  • 寻而王敦谋逆，再攻京师 - Refers to Wang Dun's subsequent rebellion and attacks on the capital.
  • 太兴四年，王敦在武昌 - Indicates the year 321 AD when Wang Dun was in Wuchang.
  • 铃下仪仗生花，如莲花 - Describes the appearance of flowers growing on the ceremonial staffs, resembling lotus flowers.
  • 五六日而萎落 - Indicates that the flowers withered and fell after a few days.
  • 易说：‘枯杨生花，何可久也’ - Refers to the *Yi Jing* (Book of Changes) saying that a withered elm tree producing flowers is not a lasting phenomenon.
  • 今狂花生枯木 - Describes the current situation where flowers are growing on a withered tree.
  • 又在铃阁之间 - Indicates that this phenomenon occurred in the ceremonial area.
  • 言威仪之富，荣华之盛，皆如狂花之发，不可久也 - Explains that the phenomenon symbolizes the temporary nature of wealth and glory.
  • 其后王敦终以逆，命加戮其尸 - Refers to the final outcome where Wang Dun was executed after his rebellion.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 舊為羽扇柄者，刻木象其骨形 - Refers to the traditional design of a feather fan handle, carved to resemble the shape of a bird's bone.
  • 列羽用十，取全數也 - Indicates that traditionally, ten feathers were used, symbolizing completeness or perfection.
  • 王敦南征，始改为长柄 - Refers to Wang Dun's southern campaign, during which the design of the fan was changed to have a longer handle.
  • 下出，可捉 - Means the longer handle made it easier to hold.
  • 改十为八，将未备夺已备也 - Suggests that reducing the number of feathers from ten to eight symbolizes taking something that is incomplete (or insufficient) and making it complete (or sufficient).
  • 识者尤之曰 - Means 'those who understand criticize it as saying.'
  • 夫羽扇，翼之名也 - A metaphorical statement that a feather fan is like a wing, suggesting it has symbolic significance.
  • 创为长柄，将执其柄以制其羽翼也 - Suggests that creating a long handle is akin to controlling the wings, implying a metaphor for control over power or influence.
  • 改十为八，将未备夺已备也 - Reiterates the idea that changing the number of feathers from ten to eight symbolizes taking something that is incomplete and making it complete.
  • 此殆敦之擅权，以制朝廷之柄 - Suggests that this change likely symbolizes Wang Dun's attempt to seize power and control the court.
  • 又将以无德之材，欲窃非据也 - Implies that Wang Dun was using someone without virtue to seize power improperly.
  • 晋明帝太宁初，武昌有大蛇 - Refers to a large snake appearing in Wuchang during the early years of Emperor Ming of Jin's reign.
  • 常居故神祠空树中，每出头从人受食 - Describes the snake living in an old shrine and emerging to eat from people.
  • 京房易传曰 - Refers to a commentary by Jing Fang on the *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
  • 蛇见于邑，不出三年，有大兵，国有大忧 - A prophecy that the appearance of a snake in a town portends war and national disaster within three years.
  • 寻有王敦之逆 - Indicates that soon after, Wang Dun rebelled.
  • 虞舜耕于历山 - Refers to the legendary Emperor Shun, who is said to have farmed at Lishan.
  • 得“玉历”于河际之岩 - Describes Shun finding the 'Jade Calendar' at the edge of a river.
  • 舜知天命在己，体道不倦 - Indicates that Shun understood that he was destined to rule and practiced the Dao diligently.
  • 舜，龙颜，大口，手握褒 - Describes Shun's physical appearance, including a dragon-like face, a large mouth, and hands holding the character '褒.'
  • 宋均注曰 - Refers to a commentary by Song Jun.
  • 握褒，手中有‘褒’字，喻从劳苦受褒饬致大祚也 - Explains that '握褒' means holding the character '褒,' symbolizing that through hard work and discipline, one can achieve great fortune.
  • 汤既克夏，大旱七年 - Refers to King Tang of Shang defeating the Xia dynasty and facing a seven-year drought.
  • 洛川竭 - Indicates that the Luo River dried up.
  • 汤乃以身祷于桑林 - Describes Tang offering himself as a sacrifice in the mulberry forest.
  • 翦其爪、发，自以为牺牲 - Means Tang cut off his claws and hair to offer himself as a sacrifice.
  • 祈福于上帝 - Indicates that Tang prayed for blessings from the Supreme Deity.
  • 于是大雨即至，洽于四海 - Describes the immediate arrival of heavy rain that spread across the land.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 羽扇 - A traditional Chinese fan made of feathers, often used in classical literature as a symbol of elegance and power.
  • 王敦 - A powerful general during the Eastern Jin dynasty who rebelled against the emperor.
  • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin, the third emperor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 武昌 - A city in present-day Hubei Province, historically significant during the Three Kingdoms and Jin dynasties.
  • 京房 - A scholar and commentator on the *Yijing* (Book of Changes) during the Han dynasty.
  • 虞舜 - The legendary Emperor Shun, a virtuous ruler in Chinese mythology.
  • 历山 - A mountain in Shandong Province, where Shun is said to have farmed.
  • 玉历 - The 'Jade Calendar,' a mythical text associated with Shun.
  • 汤 - King Tang of Shang, the founder of the Shang dynasty.
  • 洛川 - The Luo River, a major river in Henan Province.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sentence describes the traditional design of a feather fan handle, carved to resemble a bird's bone, with ten feathers symbolizing completeness.
  • This clause explains that during Wang Dun's southern campaign, the fan's handle was lengthened to make it easier to hold.
  • This phrase suggests that reducing the number of feathers from ten to eight symbolizes taking something that is incomplete and making it complete.
  • This clause introduces a critical statement by those who understand the symbolism.
  • This metaphorical statement compares a feather fan to a wing, suggesting symbolic significance.
  • This clause suggests that creating a long handle is akin to controlling the wings, implying control over power or influence.
  • This phrase reiterates the idea that changing the number of feathers from ten to eight symbolizes taking something that is incomplete and making it complete.
  • This clause suggests that this change likely symbolizes Wang Dun's attempt to seize power and control the court.
  • This clause implies that Wang Dun was using someone without virtue to seize power improperly.
  • This sentence describes a large snake appearing in Wuchang during the early years of Emperor Ming of Jin's reign, living in an old shrine and emerging to eat from people.
  • This clause refers to a commentary by Jing Fang on the *Yijing* (Book of Changes).
  • This prophecy states that the appearance of a snake in a town portends war and national disaster within three years.
  • This clause indicates that soon after, Wang Dun rebelled.
  • This sentence describes Shun farming at Lishan and finding the 'Jade Calendar' at the edge of a river.
  • This clause indicates that Shun understood that he was destined to rule and practiced the Dao diligently.
  • This description details Shun's physical appearance, including a dragon-like face, a large mouth, and hands holding the character '褒.'
  • This clause refers to a commentary by Song Jun.
  • This explanation clarifies that '握褒' means holding the character '褒,' symbolizing that through hard work and discipline, one can achieve great fortune.
  • This sentence describes King Tang of Shang defeating the Xia dynasty and facing a seven-year drought, during which the Luo River dried up.
  • This clause describes Tang offering himself as a sacrifice in the mulberry forest, cutting off his claws and hair, and praying for blessings from the Supreme Deity.
  • This clause describes the immediate arrival of heavy rain that spread across the land.

  • 王敦 - Wang Dun.
  • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin.
  • 武昌 - Wuchang.
  • 京房 - Jing Fang.
  • 虞舜 - Shun.
  • 历山 - Lishan.
  • 玉历 - Jade Calendar.
  • 汤 - Tang.
  • 洛川 - Luo River.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 非龙，非螭，非熊，非罴 - Not a dragon, not a chimeric creature, not a bear, not a wild boar. Metaphor for something extraordinary but not immediately recognizable.
  • 合得帝王师 - Will surely find a teacher to a king or emperor.
  • 赤刘当起日周亡，赤气起，火耀兴，玄丘制命，帝卯金 - A prophecy about the fall of the Zhou dynasty and the rise of a new ruler. '赤刘' refers to a future ruler, '赤气起' means red vapor arises, '火耀兴' means fire shines, '玄丘制命' means the black hill commands, and '帝卯金' means the emperor of the metal element (Jin dynasty).

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 吕望钓于渭阳 - Refers to the legendary story of Jiang Ziya (姜子牙) waiting for his chance to serve a great ruler.
  • 文王出游猎 - Refers to King Wen of Zhou, who was known for his wisdom and virtue.
  • 武王伐纣 - Refers to the historical event where King Wu of Zhou overthrew the Shang dynasty.
  • 孔子夜梦三槐之间 - Refers to a dream Confucius had, which is often interpreted as a sign of his future greatness.
  • 丰、沛之邦 - Refers to the states of Feng and Pei, which were significant during the Spring and Autumn period.
  • 吾所见一禽，如麇，羊头，头上有角，其末有肉 - Describes a mythical creature that is often associated with prophecy and divine messages.

Proper Nouns

  • 文王 - King Wen of Zhou.
  • 武王 - King Wu of Zhou.
  • 鲁哀公 - Duke Ai of Lu.
  • 孔子 - Confucius.

  • 沛 - State of Pei.
  • 赤松 - Surname of the child in the dream.
  • 时乔 - Given name of the child in the dream.
  • 受纪 - Courtesy name of the child in the dream.
  • 周 - Zhou dynasty.
  • 赤刘 - A future ruler.
  • 火耀 - Fire shines.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 孔子修春秋，制孝经 - Confucius edited the *Spring and Autumn Annals* and compiled the *Classic of Filial Piety*.
  • 斋戒向北辰而拜 - Confucius performed a ritual of fasting and offering a prayer towards the North Star as a sign of respect and devotion.
  • 告备于天 - To inform the heavens that the work was completed.
  • 洪郁，起白雾摩地 - A thick, dense fog rising from the ground.
  • 白虹自上而下，化为黄玉 - A white rainbow descending from the sky and transforming into a yellow jade.
  • 宝文出，刘季握 - An enigmatic phrase suggesting a prophecy about Liu Bang, the founder of the Han dynasty.
  • 卯，金，刀，在轸北 - A cryptic phrase that might refer to a specific constellation or astrological sign.
  • 字禾子，天下服 - Suggests that the person associated with the prophecy will have a significant impact on the world.
  • 秦穆公时，陈仓人掘地，得物 - Describes a man from Chen Cang digging and finding an unusual object.
  • 若羊非羊，若猪非猪 - Describes the object as resembling but not being a sheep or a pig.
  • 牵以献穆公 - The man brought the object to Duke Mu of Qin.
  • 道逢二童子 - The man encountered two children on the way.
  • 此名为媪 - The object is called 'Aow' (an earth spirit).
  • 常在地，食死人脑 - Describes the object as an earth spirit that feeds on the brains of the dead.
  • 若欲杀之，以柏插其首 - If one wants to kill the object, they should use a pine branch to pierce its head.
  • 彼二童子，名为陈宝 - The two children are called 'Chenbao' (a treasure).
  • 得雄者王，得雌者伯 - Suggests that whoever captures the male will become king, and whoever captures the female will become a hegemon.
  • 陈仓人舍媪逐二童子 - The man from Chen Cang abandoned the object and chased the two children.
  • 童子化为雉，飞入平林 - The children transformed into pheasants and flew into the forest.
  • 陈仓人告穆公 - The man from Chen Cang reported the incident to Duke Mu of Qin.
  • 穆公发徒大猎 - Duke Mu of Qin organized a large hunting party.
  • 果得其雌 - They indeed captured the female.
  • 又化为石 - The female transformed into a stone.
  • 置之汧、渭之间 - The stone was placed between the Qian and Wei rivers.
  • 至文公时，为立祠陈宝 - During the reign of Duke Wen of Qin, a shrine was built for Chenbao.
  • 其雄者飞至南阳 - The male flew to Nanyang.
  • 今南阳雉县，是其地也 - The current Nanyang Chixian county is where the male landed.
  • 秦欲表其符，故以名县 - The Qin dynasty wanted to mark this place as a sign of their legitimacy, so they named the county after it.
  • 每陈仓祠时有赤光 - Every time the Chen Cang shrine was worshipped, there was a red light.
  • 长十余丈 - The light was over ten zhang long (about 30 meters).
  • 从雉县来，入陈仓祠中 - The light came from Chixian county and entered the Chen Cang shrine.
  • 有声殷殷如雄雉 - There was a deep, resonant sound like that of a rooster.
  • 其后，光武起于南阳 - Later, Emperor Guangwu of Han rose to power in Nanyang.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 信不堅固 - Means 'lack of firmness in trust' or 'unstable loyalty.'
  • 邊屯守將 - Refers to military commanders stationed at frontier garrisons.
  • 質其妻子 - Means 'to hold their wives and children as hostages.'
  • 保質童子 - A term referring to the hostages, likely a specific title or designation.
  • 少年以類相與娛游者 - Means 'young men who associate and play together.'

  • 三公歸於司馬 - A prophetic statement suggesting that the Three Excellencies (high-ranking officials) will fall under the control of the Sima (a powerful family).

  • 揚州必有天子 - A prophecy that a ruler will emerge in Yangzhou.

  • 異兒 - Refers to a strange or extraordinary child.
  • 三公 - Refers to the highest-ranking officials in the government, such as the Chancellor, Imperial Counselor, and Grand Commandant.
  • 司馬 - Refers to the Sima family, which later established the Jin dynasty.

  • 人盡南渡 - Refers to the mass migration of people to the south, historically associated with the fall of the Jin dynasty.
  • 揚州 - Refers to the region of Yangzhou, a significant area in southern China.

  • 厚賂婢 - Gave the maid a large bribe, indicating a significant payment.

  • 嘗诣野王 - '嘗' means 'once' or 'formerly,' '诣' means 'to visit' or 'to go to.'
  • 主人妻夜产 - '产' means 'to give birth,' '夜' means 'at night.'
  • 俄而 - Time adverb meaning 'soon after' or 'shortly thereafter.'
  • 书之 - '书' means 'to write down' or 'to record.'
  • 十五，以兵死 - '以兵死' means 'died in battle' or 'died due to military service.'
  • 寝者为谁 - '寝者' means 'the person sleeping' or 'the sleeper.'
  • 魏公舒 - '魏公' is a title, possibly indicating a noble or official status.
  • 因条桑，为斧伤而死 - '条桑' means 'pruning mulberry trees,' '斧伤' means 'axe injury.'
  • 舒自知当为公矣 - '当为公' means 'will become a high-ranking official' or 'will attain a noble position.'
  • 贾谊为长沙王太傅 - '太傅' is a high-ranking official title.
  • 四月庚子日 - '四月' means 'April,' '庚子' is a day in the Chinese sexagenary cycle.
  • 有鹏鸟飞入其舍 - '鹏鸟' is a mythical large bird, often associated with the legend of Peng (鵬).
  • 止于坐隅 - '坐隅' means 'by the side of the seat' or 'near the seat.'
  • 良久，乃去 - '良久' means 'for a long time,' '乃去' means 'then left.'
  • 发书占之 - '发书' means 'to consult a book,' '占之' means 'to interpret' or 'to predict.'
  • 野鸟入室，主人将去 - '将去' means 'is about to leave' or 'is going to depart.'
  • 故作鵩鸟赋 - '赋' is a type of classical Chinese literary genre.
  • 齐死生而等祸福 - '齐死生' means 'to equate life and death,' '等祸福' means 'to treat misfortune and fortune equally.'
  • 致命定志焉 - '致命' means 'to dedicate one's life,' '定志' means 'to set one's mind.'
  • 王莽居摄 - '居摄' means 'to act as regent' or 'to rule in the name of another.'
  • 东郡太守翟义 - '太守' is a high-ranking local official.
  • 知其将篡汉 - '篡汉' means 'to usurp the Han dynasty.'
  • 谋举义兵 - '谋' means 'to plan,' '举义兵' means 'to raise righteous troops.'
  • 兄宣，教授诸生 - '教授' means 'to teach,' '诸生' means 'many students.'
  • 满堂 - Means 'full of students' or 'a room full of students.'
  • 群鹅雁数十在中庭 - '群' means 'a group,' '中庭' means 'the courtyard.'
  • 有狗从外入，啮之，皆死 - '啮' means 'to bite,' '皆死' means 'all died.'
  • 惊救之，皆断头 - '惊救' means 'to rush to save,' '皆断头' means 'all had their heads cut off.'
  • 狗走出门，求，不知处 - '求' means 'to search for,' '不知处' means 'unknown location.'
  • 大恶之 - '大恶' means 'greatly dislike' or 'deeply resent.'
  • 数日，莽夷其三族 - '数日' means 'after several days,' '夷其三族' means 'destroyed his three clans.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 平 - Means 'to pacify' or 'to subdue,' referring to a military campaign that ends in victory.
  • 先时 - Means 'previously' or 'beforehand,' used to introduce a sequence of events that occurred before the main narrative.
  • 数有怪 - A phrase meaning 'repeatedly had strange occurrences,' indicating a series of omens or supernatural events.
  • 一犬着冠帻，绛衣，上屋 - A literal description of a dog wearing a hat and headpiece, wearing a red robe, and climbing onto the roof. This is a supernatural omen.
  • 欻有一儿，蒸死甑中 - '欻' means 'suddenly' or 'suddenly appeared.' The phrase describes a child suddenly appearing and dying boiled in a steaming pot (甑), which is a bizarre and ominous event.
  • 生肉，长围各数尺，有头、目、口、喙，无手、足，而动摇 - Describes a piece of flesh that grows in size, with a head, eyes, mouth, and beak, but no arms or legs, and which moves. This is a grotesque omen.
  • 占者曰 - '占者' refers to a diviner or interpreter of omens.
  • 有形不成，有体无声，其国灭亡 - A prophetic saying meaning 'having form but not complete, having body but no sound—this kingdom will perish.' A standard omen formula in classical Chinese.
  • 精爽扰动 - A phrase meaning 'his spirit or vitality was disturbed,' indicating a premonition or supernatural warning.
  • 通夕不寐 - '通夕' means 'throughout the night,' and '不寐' means 'did not sleep,' indicating a sleepless night due to anxiety or premonition.
  • 犬衔引其衣 - '衔引' means 'to bite and pull,' so the dog is pulling on his clothes, a sign of warning.
  • 尔何故血臭 - '血臭' is a term that could mean 'blood smell' or 'a smell of blood,' possibly indicating that the wife sensed a violent death about to happen.
  • 汝眼目瞻视，何以不常 - The wife is questioning the maid about her unusual gaze, possibly implying she saw something supernatural or knew the truth.
  • 蹶然起跃，头至于栋 - '蹶然' means 'suddenly' or 'violently,' and '跃' means 'leapt.' The maid jumps up and hits her head on the beam.
  • 攘臂切齿而言 - '攘臂' means 'to raise one's arms,' and '切齿' means 'to gnash one's teeth,' indicating anger or fury.
  • 乃为孙峻所杀 - Indicates that the speaker is revealing the cause of death, that 诸葛恪 was killed by 孙峻.
  • 合门被诛 - '合门' means 'entire family' or 'whole household,' and '被诛' means 'was executed' or 'was killed.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 魏司马太傅懿 - Refers to 司马懿, a powerful general and later emperor of the Western Jin dynasty. '太傅' is a high-ranking official title.
  • 公孙渊 - A warlord who controlled the state of Wei in the Three Kingdoms period. He was the last ruler of the state of Wei before it fell to 曹魏.
  • 襄平 - A city in ancient Liaodong, modern-day Liaoning province. It was a key location in the conflict between the Wei and the state of Wei (controlled by 公孙渊).
  • 吴诸葛恪 - A prominent general and statesman of the Eastern Wu kingdom during the Three Kingdoms period. He was known for his military campaigns and political maneuvering.
  • 孙峻 - A powerful general and regent of the Eastern Wu kingdom who later killed 诸葛恪 in a coup.
  • 邓喜 - A military commander in the Eastern Wu kingdom.
  • 祠神 - To perform a ritual or offering to a deity or spirit.
  • 悬之 - 'To hang it,' likely referring to hanging the pig after the ritual.
  • 咋咋作声 - Describes a sound, possibly like a 'chattering' or 'choking' sound, as if the head is speaking or reacting.
  • 绕屋三日 - The head 'circled the house for three days,' indicating a supernatural phenomenon that persisted for a time.
  • 谋叛 - 'Plotting rebellion,' a serious charge in ancient China that could lead to execution of the entire family.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 伐吳 - 伐 means to attack or conquer, 吳 refers to the state of Wu; together, 伐吳 means to attack or conquer Wu.
  • 屯項城 - 屯 means to garrison or station troops, 項城 is a place name; together, 屯項城 means to station troops at Xiangcheng.
  • 忽失充所在 - 忽 means suddenly, 失 means to lose, 所在 means location; together, 忽失充所在 means suddenly lost track of where Jia Chong was.
  • 周勤時昼寝 - 昼寝 means to nap during the day; together, 周勤時昼寝 means Zhou Qin was napping during the day.
  • 百余人 - Literally more than a hundred people, used here to describe a large group.
  • 录充引入一径 - 录 means to lead or guide, 引入 means to lead into, 一径 means a path; together, 录充引入一径 means led Jia Chong into a path.
  • 侍卫甚盛 - 侍卫 means guards or attendants, 甚盛 means very numerous; together, 侍卫甚盛 means with many guards.
  • 府公南面坐 - 府公 refers to a high-ranking official, 南面坐 means to sit facing south, which is a sign of authority.
  • 将乱吾家事者，必尔与荀勖 - 将 means will, 乱 means to disrupt, 吾家事 means my family affairs, 必 means certainly, 尔 means you, 荀勖 is a proper name; together, 将乱吾家事者，必尔与荀勖 means those who will disrupt my family affairs are certainly you and Xun Xu.
  • 惑吾子 - 惑 means to bewitch or mislead, 吾子 means my son; together, 惑吾子 means misled my son.
  • 乱吾孙 - 乱 means to disrupt, 吾孙 means my grandson; together, 乱吾孙 means disrupted my grandson.
  • 间使任恺黜汝而不去 - 间 means secretly, 使 means to make, 任恺 is a proper name, 黜 means to demote, 汝 means you; together, 间使任恺黜汝而不去 means secretly made Ren Kai demote you but you did not leave.
  • 使庾纯詈汝而不改 - 使 means to make, 庾纯 is a proper name, 詈 means to curse, 改 means to change; together, 使庾纯詈汝而不改 means made Yu Chun curse you but you did not change.
  • 吴寇当平 - 吴寇 means the enemy from Wu, 当平 means will be pacified; together, 吴寇当平 means the enemy from Wu will be pacified.
  • 汝方表斩张华 - 汝 means you, 方 means just now, 表 means to propose, 斩张华 means to execute Zhang Hua; together, 汝方表斩张华 means you just proposed to execute Zhang Hua.
  • 汝之暗戆 - 暗 means stupid, 戆 means stubborn; together, 汝之暗戆 means your stupidity and stubbornness.
  • 系嗣死于钟虞之间 - 系嗣 means heir, 死 means to die, 于 means in, 钟虞之间 is a place name; together, 系嗣死于钟虞之间 means the heir will die between Zhong and Yu.
  • 小子困于枯木之下 - 小子 means young son, 困 means to be trapped, 于 means under, 枯木 means dead wood; together, 小子困于枯木之下 means the young son will be trapped under dead wood.
  • 荀勖亦宜同然 - 亦 means also, 宜 means should, 同然 means the same; together, 荀勖亦宜同然 means Xun Xu should also be the same.
  • 其先德小浓 - 其 means his, 先德 means virtue of ancestors, 小浓 means slightly thick; together, 其先德小浓 means his ancestors' virtue is slightly thick.
  • 国嗣亦替 - 国嗣 means the heir to the throne, 亦 means also, 替 means to decline; together, 国嗣亦替 means the heir to the throne will also decline.
  • 言毕命去 - 言毕 means after speaking, 命去 means ordered to leave; together, 言毕命去 means after speaking, ordered to leave.
  • 充忽然得还营 - 充 refers to Jia Chong, 忽然 means suddenly, 得 means managed to, 还营 means return to the camp; together, 充忽然得还营 means Jia Chong suddenly managed to return to the camp.
  • 颜色憔悴，性理昏错 - 颜色憔悴 means appearance worn out, 性理昏错 means mental state confused; together, 颜色憔悴，性理昏错 means appearance worn out, mental state confused.
  • 至后，谧死于钟下，贾后服金酒而死，贾午考竟用大杖终 - 至后 means later, 谧 is a proper name, 死于钟下 means died at the place called Zhong, 贾后 is a proper name, 服金酒而死 means died from poisoned wine, 贾午 is a proper name, 考竟用大杖终 means died from being beaten with a stick; together, 至后，谧死于钟下，贾后服金酒而死，贾午考竟用大杖终 means later, Mi died at the place called Zhong, Jia Hou died from poisoned wine, and Jia Wu died from being beaten with a stick.

  • 有若钟乳状 - Comparison suggesting something crystalline or precious.

  • 于字，‘禾’‘失’为‘秩’ - Wordplay suggesting that the dreamer will receive a high rank or salary.

  • 此人相贫，限不过此 - Literally 'this person's destiny is poverty, limited to this,' suggesting a predestined fate of poverty.
  • 惟有张车子，应赐录千万 - Literally 'only Zhang Cheli should be given a record of ten thousand,' indicating a specific individual who is fated to receive wealth.
  • 车子未生，请以借之 - Literally 'Zhang Cheli has not been born, please lend it to him,' suggesting a transfer of fate or wealth to another.
  • 曙觉，言之 - Literally 'woke up in the dawn, spoke about it,' indicating the sharing of the dream.
  • 戮力 - Literally 'unite efforts,' meaning to work together.
  • 昼夜治生 - Literally 'all day and night, manage one's livelihood,' indicating relentless work to improve one's life.
  • 赀至千万 - Literally 'wealth reached ten thousand,' indicating significant accumulation of wealth.
  • 野合 - Literally 'wild union,' referring to an unconventional or illicit relationship.
  • 月满，当孕 - Literally 'when the moon was full, she was pregnant,' indicating the timing of conception.
  • 驻车屋下 - Literally 'stopped under the carriage house,' suggesting a specific location.
  • 主人往视 - Literally 'the master went to see,' indicating the owner's involvement.
  • 哀其孤寒 - Literally 'pitied his loneliness and poverty,' indicating sympathy for someone's difficult situation.
  • 作粥糜食之 - Literally 'made porridge to feed him,' indicating care and provision.
  • 当名汝儿作何 - Literally 'what should you name your child,' indicating a question about naming.
  • 梦天告之 - Literally 'dreamed that the Heavenly One told him,' indicating a divine message.
  • 财当归之矣 - Literally 'wealth should return to him,' indicating a change in fortune.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 進南書佐劉雅 - Refers to Liu Ya, who was promoted to the position of *Shuzuo* (a type of official) in the southern region.
  • 夢見青刺蜴從屋落其腹内 - A literal description of a dream where a green spiny lizard falls into Liu Ya’s abdomen.
  • 因苦腹痛病 - Indicates that Liu Ya subsequently suffers from abdominal pain.
  • 後漢張奂為武威太守 - Refers to Zhang Huan, who served as the governor of Wuwei during the Later Han period.
  • 其妻夢帝與印綬，登樓而歌 - Describes a dream where Zhang Huan’s wife sees the emperor giving her a seal and sash, and she climbs a tower to sing.
  • 覺，以告奂 - Indicates that upon waking, she tells Zhang Huan about the dream.
  • 令占之 - Zhang Huan asks for an interpretation of the dream.
  • 夫人方生男，后臨此郡命終此樓 - The interpreter predicts that his wife will give birth to a son who will later die in the same tower.
  • 建安中，果為武威太守殺刺史 - Refers to the period of Jian’an, when Liu Yong (the son) became the governor of Wuwei and killed the governor (刺史).
  • 邯鄲商州兵圍急 - Describes a situation where the army of Shangdang besieges Liu Yong.
  • 猛耻见擒，乃登楼自焚而死 - Indicates that Liu Yong, feeling ashamed of being captured, climbs the tower and commits suicide by fire.
  • 漢靈帝梦见桓帝 - Describes a dream where Emperor Ling of Han sees Emperor Yuan (Huan Di).
  • 怒曰：“宋皇后有何罪过，而听用邪孽，使绝其命。” - Emperor Yuan expresses anger over the execution of Empress Song.
  • 渤海王悝，既已自贬，又受诛毙 - Refers to Prince Ku of Bohai, who was forced to abdicate and later executed.
  • 宋氏及悝，自诉于天，上帝震怒，罪在难救 - Indicates that the spirits of Empress Song and Prince Ku are petitioning heaven, and the gods are angry, making their punishment inevitable.
  • 吴时嘉兴徐伯始病 - Refers to Xu Bishi, who fell ill during the Wu period in Jiaxing.
  • 使道士吕石安神座 - Xu Bishi invites Taoist priest Lu Shi to perform a ritual.
  • 石有弟子戴本、王思 - Lu Shi has disciples named Dai Ben and Wang Si.
  • 三人居住海盐，伯始迎之以助石 - Xu Bishi invites Dai Ben and Wang Si from Haisan to assist Lu Shi.
  • 昼卧，梦上天北斗门下见外鞍马三匹 - During the day, Xu Bishi dreams of three saddled horses at the North Star gate.
  • 云：“明日当以一迎石，一迎本，一迎思。” - The dream foretells that one horse will be for Lu Shi, one for Dai Ben, and one for Wang Si.
  • 石梦觉，语本、思云：“如此死期，可急还，与家别。” - Lu Shi wakes up and tells Dai Ben and Wang Si that they are facing death and should return home.
  • 不卒事而去 - Dai Ben and Wang Si leave without completing their task.
  • 伯始怪而留之 - Xu Bishi is surprised and keeps them.
  • 曰：“惧不得见家也。” - They explain that they are afraid they won’t see their families again.
  • 间一日，三人同时死 - A day later, all three die simultaneously.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 会稽 - Historical prefecture in Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
  • 永嘉 - Historical prefecture in Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
  • 樗蒲 - Ancient Chinese dice game popular during the Han and Tang dynasties.
  • 水神 - In Chinese mythology, water deities are often associated with rivers, lakes, and oceans, and can be invoked in various rituals and beliefs.
  • 堕水而死 - Drowning was a common form of death in ancient times, often attributed to divine intervention or supernatural causes.
  • 许 - The use of '许' to indicate a place where someone was met or lived is a common practice in classical Chinese literature.
  • 良久 - This phrase is used to indicate a passage of time, often emphasizing the duration of an event or action.
  • 的的 - This emphatic adverb is used to stress the accuracy or precision of a statement.
  • 如厕 - The act of using the bathroom is mentioned in the context of a sudden illness or death, reflecting the immediacy of the situation.
  • 凶具 - Funeral preparations were a crucial part of ancient Chinese funerary practices, involving various items and rituals.
  • 幼丧父母 - Losing parents at a young age was a common theme in classical Chinese literature, often highlighting themes of filial piety and resilience.
  • 叔父 - The relationship between an uncle and a nephew was highly valued in ancient Chinese society, with uncles often playing a significant role in the upbringing of their nephews.
  • 三更中 - The third watch of the night was a time of darkness and stillness, often associated with dreams and supernatural occurrences.
  • 强逼 - The concept of forcing or compelling someone to do something is a common theme in classical Chinese literature, often used to explore moral and ethical dilemmas.
  • 念汝能事叔父 - Filial piety, or the respect and care for one's elders, was a fundamental virtue in ancient Chinese culture.
  • 当为汝活之 - The idea of saving someone for the sake of another person reflects the deep familial and social bonds in ancient Chinese society.
  • 觉 - The act of waking up or becoming aware is used to transition between the dream and waking states, highlighting the blurring of boundaries between the two.
  • 差 - Recovery from illness or improvement in health was a significant concern in ancient Chinese medicine and daily life.

  • 唱而不和，動而不隨 - If one sings and no one responds, or if one moves and no one follows, illustrating the consequences of insincerity or lack of harmony.

  • 虛發而下鳥 - To shoot and make the bird fall without actually hitting it, describing the archer's skill.

  • 鼋衔左骖，没之 - '鼋' is a giant turtle, '衔' means 'bit,' '左骖' refers to the left side horse, and '没之' means 'drowned it.'
  • 邪行五里，逆行三里 - '邪行' means 'went sideways' or 'swerved,' and '逆行' means 'backtracked.'
  • 燕跃鹄踊而出 - '燕跃' means 'leaped like a swallow,' and '鹄踊' means 'jumped like a swan.'
  • 水为逆流三百步 - '水为逆流' means 'the water flowed backward,' and '三百步' means 'three hundred steps.'
  • 楚干将莫邪为楚王作剑 - '干将莫邪' are the names of two famous swordsmiths, and '作剑' means 'made swords.'
  • 去时嘱我 - '去时' means 'when he left,' and '嘱我' means 'entrusted me with a message.'
  • 告之曰 - '告之' means 'told him,' and '曰' means 'that.'
  • 吾父所在 - '吾父' means 'my father,' and '所在' means 'where he is.'
  • 吾为王作剑，三年乃成 - '吾为王作剑' means 'I made the sword for the king,' and '三年乃成' means 'it took three years to complete.'
  • 王怒，往，必杀我 - '王怒' means 'the king was angry,' '往' means 'to go,' and '必杀我' means 'he will definitely kill me.'
  • 出户，往南山，松生石上，剑在其背 - '出户' means 'go out the door,' '往南山' means 'to the south mountain,' '松生石上' means 'a pine tree growing on a rock,' and '剑在其背' means 'the sword is on its back.'
  • 王梦见一儿，眉间广尺，言欲报雠 - '王梦见一儿' means 'the king dreamed of a boy,' '眉间广尺' means 'with a broad forehead,' and '言欲报雠' means 'who said he wanted to take revenge.'

  • 长沙所属蛮县 - Refers to a county in the area under the jurisdiction of Changsha, likely inhabited by the '蛮' (Mán) people, a term used for various non-Han ethnic groups.
  • 亭长 - A local official in ancient China, typically responsible for maintaining order in a small district.
  • 化为虎 - The ability to transform into a tiger is a common motif in Chinese mythology, often associated with shapeshifting deities or spirits.
  • 貙 - A mythical creature that can transform into a human, often depicted as wearing a purple cotton robe and having feet without heels.

Proper Nouns

  • 江，汉之域 - The regions along the Yangtze and Han rivers.
  • 廪君 - A legendary leader from the ancient Ba people.
  • 长沙所属蛮县 - A county in the area under the jurisdiction of Changsha.
  • 亭长 - A local official in ancient China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蜀中西南高山之上 - Refers to a specific geographical location in southwestern Shu (ancient Sichuan).
  • 与猴相类 - Means 'similar to monkeys,' indicating a primate-like creature.
  • 能作人行，善走逐人 - Describes the creature's ability to walk like a human and chase people.
  • 伺道行妇女有美者 - Means 'wait for women traveling on the road who are beautiful,' indicating the creature's behavior.
  • 辄盗取，将去 - Means 'then steal and take away,' describing the creature's actions.
  • 若取得人女，则为家室 - Means 'if it takes a human woman, it will make her a wife,' indicating the creature's reproductive behavior.
  • 其无子者，终身不得还 - Means 'those without children cannot return for life,' describing the fate of the women taken by the creature.
  • 十年之后，形皆类之 - Means 'after ten years, their appearance becomes similar to the creature,' indicating a transformation over time.
  • 意亦迷惑，不复思归 - Means 'they also become confused and no longer think of returning,' describing the psychological state of the women.
  • 若有子者，辄抱送还其家 - Means 'if they have children, they will be carried back to their families,' indicating the creature's behavior towards women who have children.
  • 产子，皆如人形 - Means 'the children they produce are all human-like,' describing the offspring.
  • 有不养者，其母辄死 - Means 'if they do not raise the children, the mother will die,' indicating a form of punishment or natural consequence.
  • 故惧怕之，无敢不养 - Means 'therefore, they are afraid and dare not neglect the children,' describing the fear and compliance of the women.
  • 及长，与人不异 - Means 'when they grow up, they are no different from humans,' indicating the final state of the offspring.
  • 皆以杨为姓 - Means 'all are surnamed Yang,' indicating the naming convention of the offspring.
  • 故今蜀中西南多诸杨 - Means 'therefore, there are many people surnamed Yang in southwestern Shu today,' linking the historical account to the present.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蜀中西南高山之上 - Refers to the mountainous regions of southwestern Sichuan, known for its rich mythology and folklore.
  • 伺道行妇女有美者 - This behavior is often associated with supernatural or mythological creatures in Chinese folklore.
  • 若取得人女，则为家室 - This practice is similar to the concept of 'wild men' or 'wild women' in various mythologies, where such creatures take human partners.
  • 其无子者，终身不得还 - This reflects a common theme in folklore where supernatural beings impose conditions on their human captives.
  • 十年之后，形皆类之 - This transformation over time is a common motif in Chinese mythology, where humans can change into other forms or beings.
  • 皆以杨为姓 - The surname 'Yang' is a common Chinese surname, and this passage suggests a mythological origin for its prevalence in the region.

Proper Nouns

  • 蜀中 - Refers to the ancient region of Shu, which is modern-day Sichuan.
  • 猳国 - A kingdom of apes in Chinese mythology.
  • 马化 - A term meaning 'horse transformation.'
  • 玃猿 - An ape with long arms.
  • 杨 - A common Chinese surname.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 临川间诸山有妖物 - Describes a supernatural entity inhabiting the mountains near the city of Linchuan.
  • 来常因大风雨 - Indicates that the entity appears during violent storms.
  • 有声如啸 - Describes the sound it makes, likened to a howl.
  • 能射人 - Suggests that the entity can shoot at people.
  • 其所着者，有顷，便肿，大毒 - Describes the effects of being hit by the entity, causing swelling and severe poisoning.
  • 有雌雄：雄急，而雌缓 - Indicates that the entity has male and female forms, with different speeds of effect.
  • 急者不过半日间，缓者经宿 - Describes the duration of the effects, with the male form acting quickly and the female form slowly.
  • 其旁人常有以救之 - Suggests that there are methods to save those affected.
  • 救之少迟，则死 - Warns that if the rescue is delayed, the victim will die.
  • 俗名曰“刀劳鬼” - Refers to the common name given to this entity, 'Dao Laogui'.
  • 故外书云：“鬼神者，其祸福发扬之验于世者也。” - Quotes an external source that defines ghosts and spirits as manifestations of fortune and misfortune in the world.
  • 老子曰：“昔之得一者：天得一以清，地得一以宁，神得一以灵，谷得一以盈，侯王得一以为天下贞。” - Quotes Laozi, stating that the universe and its components achieve their optimal states through unity.
  • 然则天地鬼神，与我并生者也 - Suggests that heaven, earth, ghosts, and spirits coexist with humans.
  • 气分则性异，域别则形殊 - Explains that different essences lead to different natures, and different realms result in different forms.
  • 莫能相兼也 - Indicates that these entities cannot coexist or be combined.
  • 生者主阳，死者主阴 - Suggests that living beings are associated with yang (positive energy), while the dead are associated with yin (negative energy).
  • 性之所托，各安其生 - Suggests that each entity finds its place and lives according to its nature.
  • 太阴之中，怪物存焉 - Indicates that in the realm of yin, there are strange and monstrous entities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 临川 - Refers to the city of Linchuan, located in present-day Jiangxi Province, China.
  • 刀劳鬼 - A local name for a supernatural entity known for causing harm and illness.
  • 老子 - Refers to Laozi, the founder of Daoism and author of the *Tao Te Ching*.
  • 天得一以清，地得一以宁，神得一以灵，谷得一以盈，侯王得一以为天下贞 - A famous passage from the *Tao Te Ching* that emphasizes the importance of unity in achieving harmony and balance.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This long sequence describes the characteristics and effects of the supernatural entity.
  • This clause explains the differences between the male and female forms of the entity.
  • This clause describes the methods of rescue and the consequences of delay.
  • This phrase introduces the common name for the entity.
  • This quote provides a definition of ghosts and spirits.
  • This quote from Laozi emphasizes the importance of unity.
  • This clause explains the coexistence and differences between various entities.
  • This final clause describes the relationship between life and death, and the existence of strange entities in the realm of yin.

Proper Nouns

  • 临川 - The city of Linchuan.
  • 刀劳鬼 - The common name for the supernatural entity.
  • 老子 - Laozi, the founder of Daoism.
  • 天 - Heaven.
  • 地 - Earth.
  • 神 - Spirits.
  • 谷 - Valleys.
  • 侯王 - Rulers or kings.
  • 太阴 - The realm of yin.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 越地深山中有鸟 - Literal description of a bird found in the deep mountains of Yue (Yue territory).
  • 大如鸠 - Describes the bird's size, comparing it to a jay (鸠).
  • 青色 - Describes the bird's color as greenish-blue.
  • 名曰“冶鸟” - The bird is named '冶鸟' (冶鸟), a proper name.
  • 穿大树，作巢 - Describes the bird's nesting behavior, digging into large trees to build nests.
  • 如五六升器 - Describes the nest's size, roughly equivalent to a container holding five or six sheng (a unit of volume).
  • 户口径数寸 - Describes the entrance to the nest, with a diameter of several inches.
  • 周饰以土垭 - Describes the nest's decoration with earth and clay.
  • 赤白相分 - Describes the nest's coloration, alternating between red and white.
  • 状如射侯 - Describes the nest's appearance, resembling a target (射侯).
  • 伐木者见此树，即避之去 - Describes the behavior of woodcutters who avoid trees with these nests.
  • 或夜冥不见鸟，鸟亦知人不见，便鸣唤曰：“咄咄上去！” - Describes the bird's behavior when it senses a person nearby at night, calling out to warn them to go up.
  • 明日便宜急上 - Advises the woodcutter to climb the tree quickly the next day.
  • 咄咄下去 - Describes the bird's call to warn the woodcutter to go down.
  • 明日便宜急下 - Advises the woodcutter to climb down quickly the next day.
  • 若不使去，但言笑而不已者，人可止伐也 - Describes the bird's behavior when it does not want the woodcutter to leave, laughing persistently until the woodcutter stops.
  • 若有秽恶及其所止者，则有虎通夕来守 - Describes the bird's protective behavior, with tigers guarding the nest if it is defiled.
  • 人不去，便伤害人 - Describes the consequences if the woodcutter does not leave, as the tigers will harm them.
  • 此鸟，白日见其形，是鸟也；夜听其鸣，亦鸟也 - Describes the bird's dual nature, appearing as a bird during the day and making bird-like sounds at night.
  • 时有观乐者，便作人形，长三尺，至涧中取石蟹 - Describes the bird's ability to transform into a human-like form to fetch stone crabs from a stream.
  • 就人炙之，人不可犯也 - Describes the bird's interaction with humans, roasting the crabs and warning humans not to interfere.
  • 越人谓此鸟是“越祝”之祖也 - Describes the bird's significance in Yue culture, being considered the ancestor of the '越祝' (Yue shaman).

Proper Nouns

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 南海之外 - Literally 'beyond the South Sea,' used to introduce a location outside the known world.

  • 不废织绩 - Means 'does not stop weaving,' indicating that the creature continues to engage in weaving activities.
  • 其眼，泣，则能出珠 - Means 'when its eyes weep, it can produce pearls,' describing a unique ability of the creature.
  • 庐江耽，枞阳二县境 - Refers to the regions of Lujiang and Zongyang, indicating the geographical area where the events take place.
  • 时闻哭声多者至数十人 - Means 'often heard crying sounds, sometimes as many as dozens of people,' describing the frequency and intensity of the sounds.
  • 男女大小，如始丧者 - Means 'men, women, young and old, as if they were just bereaved,' describing the appearance of the people.
  • 邻人惊骇，至彼奔赴，常不见人 - Means 'neighbors were startled and rushed there, but often did not see anyone,' describing the reaction of the neighbors.
  • 然于哭地，必有死丧 - Means 'but at the place where they cried, there was always a death,' linking the crying sounds to actual deaths.
  • 率声若多，则为大家；声若小，则为小家 - Means 'generally, if the sound was loud, it was for a large family; if it was soft, it was for a small family,' describing the correlation between the volume of the sound and the size of the family.
  • 庐江大山之间 - Refers to the large mountains of Lujiang, indicating the location where the '山都' live.
  • 似人，裸身，见人便走 - Means 'resembling humans, naked, and running away when they see people,' describing the appearance and behavior of the '山都.'
  • 可长四五丈 - Means 'can grow up to four or five zhang tall,' indicating the size of the '山都.'
  • 常在幽昧之中，似魑魅鬼物 - Means 'often in dark and obscure places, resembling ghosts and spirits,' describing their habitat and appearance.
  • 汉光武中平中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Guangwu during the Zhongping era, indicating the historical period.
  • 有物处于江水，其名曰“蜮，”一曰“短狐。” - Means 'there was something in the river, known as 'yù' or 'short fox',' describing the creature.
  • 能含沙射人 - Means 'can shoot people with sand,' describing the creature's ability to harm people.
  • 所中者，则身体筋急，头痛，发热。剧者至死 - Means 'those who are hit experience muscle stiffness, headache, fever, and severe cases can lead to death,' describing the effects of the creature's attack.
  • 江人以术方抑之，则得沙石于肉中 - Means 'people from the river use techniques to suppress it, and sand and stones are found in the flesh,' describing the method to treat the effects.
  • 诗所谓“为鬼，为蜮，”则不可测也 - Means 'as the poem says, 'they are ghosts or yù,' they are unpredictable,' linking the creature to poetic descriptions.
  • 今俗谓之“溪毒。” - Means 'nowadays it is called 'river poison,' providing the current name for the creature.
  • 先儒以为男女同川而浴，淫女，为主乱气所生也 - Means 'ancient scholars believed that it was caused by men and women bathing in the same river, with promiscuous women being the source of disorder,' providing a cultural explanation for the creature's origin.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 永昌郡不違縣 - Geographical reference, meaning 'the county of Buiwu in Yongchang Prefecture.'
  • 禁水 - Literally 'forbidden water,' referring to a river or body of water with toxic properties.
  • 有毒氣 - Means 'toxic gas' or 'poisonous vapor.'
  • 唯十一月，十三月差可渡涉 - Specific time frame, 'only in the 11th and 13th months' (likely referring to the lunar calendar), when it is somewhat safe to cross the river.
  • 自正月至十月不可渡 - Means 'from the 1st to the 10th months' (again, likely lunar months), when it is unsafe to cross.
  • 渡辄病杀人 - Means 'crossing causes illness and death.'
  • 其气中有恶物 - Refers to 'something evil' in the air, likely a metaphor for the toxic gas.
  • 不见其形，其似有声 - Means 'it cannot be seen, but it seems to make a sound.'
  • 如有所投击内中木，则折 - Means 'if something is thrown into the middle of the water, the wood will break.'
  • 中人，则害 - Means 'if it hits a person, it will harm them.'
  • 士俗号为“鬼弹” - Refers to the local nickname 'ghost bullet' given to the phenomenon.
  • 故郡有罪人，徙之禁防，不过十日，皆死 - Means 'therefore, criminals from the prefecture are exiled to the forbidden area, and within ten days, they all die.'
  • 余外妇姊夫蒋士，有佣客，得疾，下血 - Refers to a personal anecdote about a relative, 'my sister-in-law's husband, Jiang Shi, had a hired guest who fell ill and bled.'
  • 医以中蛊 - Means 'the doctor diagnosed it as a case of poisoning by a venomous creature.'
  • 乃密以蘘荷根布席下 - Means 'he secretly placed the roots of the *náng hé* (a type of herb) under the mat.'
  • 不使知 - Means 'without the patient knowing.'
  • 乃狂言曰：“食我虫者，乃张小小也。” - Means 'he then spoke wildly, saying, 'The one who ate my worm is Zhang Xiaoxiao.''
  • 今世攻蛊，多用蘘荷根，往往验 - Means 'in modern times, treating poisonings often involves using *náng hé* roots, and it is often effective.'
  • 蘘荷，或谓嘉草 - Refers to *náng hé*, which is also called 'good grass' (*jiā cǎo*).
  • 鄱阳赵寿，有犬，蛊 - Refers to a personal anecdote about a dog owned by Zhao Shou from Poyang, which was also affected by poisoning.
  • 时陈岑诣寿 - Means 'when Chen Cen visited Zhao Shou.'
  • 忽有大黄犬六七群，出吠岑 - Means 'suddenly, six or seven groups of large yellow dogs appeared and barked at Chen Cen.'
  • 后余相伯归与寿妇食，吐血，几死 - Refers to another personal anecdote about a relative, 'later, my uncle returned home with Zhao Shou's wife and they ate together, and she vomited blood and nearly died.'
  • 乃屑桔梗以饮之而愈 - Means 'he then ground up *qi ji* (a type of herb) and gave it to her to drink, and she recovered.'
  • 蛊有怪物，若鬼 - Refers to 'poisonous creatures' that are described as 'monsters like ghosts.'
  • 其妖形变化杂类殊种 - Means 'their strange forms change into various kinds of creatures.'
  • 或为狗豕，或为虫蛇 - Means 'sometimes they appear as dogs or pigs, sometimes as insects or snakes.'
  • 其人不自知其形状 - Means 'the person does not know their own form.'
  • 行之于百姓，所中皆死 - Means 'they act among the people, and those who are affected all die.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 永昌郡 - A historical prefecture in Yunnan, China, during the Han dynasty.

  • 蛊 - A term referring to poisoning by venomous creatures, a common theme in Chinese folklore and medicine.

  • 鄱阳 - A place name in Jiangxi province, China.
  • 陈岑 - A personal name, possibly a historical figure or a fictional character in the context of the anecdote.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 徙南岳之祭 - Relocation of the Southern Mountain (Nanyue) sacrifices.
  • 灊县，霍山之上 - Refers to Qian County and Mount Huoshan, the new location of the sacrifices.
  • 四镬 - Literally 'four pots,' but in this context, likely refers to four ritual vessels or cauldrons.

  • 事毕，即空 - Once the ritual was completed, it would become empty, indicating the water would disappear.
  • 尘土树叶，莫之污也 - Neither dust nor leaves could defile it, suggesting the water was pure and untouched by external elements.
  • 积五十岁 - Accumulated over fifty years, indicating the duration of this phenomenon.
  • 岁作四祭 - Four sacrifices were held each year.
  • 后但作三祭 - Later, only three sacrifices were held, indicating a change in the ritual.
  • 一镬自败 - One pot broke on its own, suggesting a natural or supernatural occurrence.
  • 樊东之口，有樊山 - Refers to 'the mouth of Fan East, where there is Fan Mountain.'

  • 空乘之地，今名为孔宝 - The place called Kongbao, which is now named after a hole or cavern.
  • 鲁南，山之穴外，有双石，如桓楹起立，高数丈 - Two stones outside a cave in the south of Lu, standing like pillars, several zhang tall.

  • 洒扫以告 - Cleaning and announcing, referring to the ritual preparation.

  • 穴淹，则大雨立至 - Once the cave was blocked, heavy rain would immediately arrive, indicating the success of the ritual.
  • 秦惠王二十七年 - The 27th year of King Hui of Qin's reign.
  • 使张仪筑成都城 - He ordered Zhang Yi to build the city of Chengdu.

  • 忽有大龟浮于江，至东子城东南隅而毙 - Suddenly, a large turtle floated down the river and died at the southeast corner of the eastern city, describing a miraculous event.

  • 便就，故名龟化城 - It was completed, and thus named the Turtle Transformation City, indicating the city's name and origin.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 土蜂，名曰“蜾●，”今世謂“●●，”“細腰”之類 - Describes the earth wasp, known by different names and classified as a type of wasp with a slender waist.
  • 其為物雄而無雌，不交，不產 - Describes the wasp as male-only, not mating, and not laying eggs.
  • 常取桑蟲或阜螽子育之，則皆化成己子 - States that the wasp often takes silkworms or locust eggs to raise, which transform into the wasp's offspring.
  • 亦或謂之“螟蛉” - Another name for the earth wasp, also referred to as 'mengling'.
  • 詩曰：“螟蛉有子，果羸負之，”是也 - Refers to a line from the *Book of Songs* that mentions the wasp adopting the larvae of other insects.
  • 木蠹，生蟲，羽化為蝶 - Describes how woodworms transform into butterflies.
  • 猬多刺，故不使超踰楊柳 - Explains that hedgehogs have many spines, which prevent them from jumping over willows.
  • 昆仑之(山虚)，地首也，是惟帝之下都 - Describes Mount Kunlun as the 'empty mountain', the head of the earth, and the lower capital of the emperor.
  • 故其外绝以弱水之深，又环以炎火之山 - States that beyond the mountain lies the deep waters of the Weak Water, and it is surrounded by the fiery mountains.
  • 山上有鸟兽草木，皆生育滋长于炎火之中 - Describes the birds, animals, and plants on the mountain, all growing in the midst of fire.
  • 汉世西域旧献此布，中闲久绝 - Mentions that during the Han dynasty, the Western Regions used to offer this cloth, but it had been absent for a long time.
  • 至魏初时，人疑其无有 - States that by the early Wei dynasty, people doubted its existence.
  • 文帝以为火性酷裂，无含生之气，着之典论，明其不然之事，绝智者之听 - Emperor Wen of Wei believed that fire is harsh and destructive, without life-generating properties, and wrote about this in his *Classic Treatise* to show that it was impossible, thus silencing the wise.
  • 及明帝立，诏三公曰：“先帝昔着典论，不朽之格言，其刊石于庙门之外及太学，与“石经”并以永示来世。” - When Emperor Ming ascended the throne, he ordered the Three Excellencies to inscribe the *Classic Treatise* on stone outside the temple and at the Imperial Academy, alongside the 'Stone Classics', to be shown to future generations.
  • 至是，西域使人献“火浣布”袈裟，于是刊灭此论，而天下笑之 - Eventually, envoys from the Western Regions presented 'fire-washed cloth' robes, leading to the destruction of the *Classic Treatise*, and people laughed at the emperor's earlier skepticism.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 夫金之性一也 - Classical opening formula meaning 'The nature of metal is uniform.'
  • 以五月丙午日中铸 - Refers to casting a metal object at noon on the day of Bingwu (丙午) in the fifth month.
  • 为阳燧 - Refers to casting a 'yang mirror,' an ancient tool used to focus sunlight to start a fire.
  • 以十一月壬子夜半铸 - Refers to casting a metal object at midnight on the day of Renzi (壬子) in the eleventh month.
  • 汉灵帝时 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Ling of Han.
  • 陈留蔡邕 - Refers to Cai Yong, a scholar from Chenliu.
  • 以数上书陈奏，忤上旨意 - Means 'because he repeatedly submitted memorials that offended the emperor's wishes.'
  • 又内宠恶之 - Means 'and was further disliked by the emperor's favorites.'
  • 虑不免，乃亡命江海，远迹吴会 - Means 'fearing he could not escape punishment, he fled to the rivers and seas, distancing himself to Wu and Kuaiji.'
  • 至吴，吴人有烧桐以爨者 - Means 'When he arrived in Wu, there was a Wu person who was burning a paulownia log for cooking.'
  • 因请之，削以为琴，果有美音 - Means 'He asked for it, had it carved into a zither, and indeed it produced beautiful music.'
  • 蔡邕尝至柯亭 - Means 'Cai Yong once went to Keting.'
  • 以竹为椽 - Means 'He used bamboo for the rafters.'
  • 取以为笛，发声辽亮 - Means 'He took it to make a flute, which produced a clear and resonant sound.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 汉灵帝 - Refers to Emperor Ling of Han (156–189 AD), the penultimate emperor of the Eastern Han dynasty.
  • 陈留蔡邕 - Refers to Cai Yong (132–192 AD), a renowned scholar, musician, and calligrapher from Chenliu (modern-day Henan).
  • 阳燧 - An ancient tool used to focus sunlight to start a fire, symbolizing the harnessing of natural forces.
  • 阴燧 - An ancient tool used to collect dew or moisture, symbolizing the collection of subtle energies.
  • 焦尾琴 - A famous zither made from a charred paulownia log, named for its unique appearance and sound.
  • 柯亭 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in ancient China.
  • 会稽高迁亭 - A historical site in Kuaiji (modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang), where Cai Yong is said to have discovered the bamboo for his flute.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 昔高阳氏 - In ancient times, the GaoYang clan.
  • 同产而为夫妇 - Born as siblings but became husband and wife.
  • 相抱而死 - Embraced each other and died.
  • 神鸟以不死草覆之 - A divine bird covered them with the herb of immortality.
  • 七年，男女同体而生 - After seven years, they were born as a hermaphrodite.
  • 二头，四手足 - Two heads, four hands, and four feet.
  • 蒙双氏 - The MengShuang clan or The Twin-Headed Clan.

  • 得耳疾 - Suffered from an ear ailment.

  • 赠金千斤，封邑万户 - Reward with a thousand catties of gold and grant a fief of ten thousand households.
  • 又赐以少女 - And also give a young maiden as a reward.

  • 少女闻之 - The young maiden heard this.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 于是女解去衣裳 - 女解去衣裳 means the woman undressed.
  • 为仆竖之结 - 仆竖 likely refers to a servant or attendant, and 结 means knot or tie. This phrase is ambiguous but could mean she tied herself up like a servant.
  • 着独力之衣 - 独力 could mean self-reliant or single-handed, but in this context, it might refer to a specific type of garment.
  • 随盘瓠升山，入谷，止于石室之中 - 升山，入谷 means ascended the mountain, entered the valley, and 止于石室之中 means stopped in a cave.
  • 王悲思之，遣往视觅 - 悲思之 means mourned for her, and 遣往视觅 means sent to look for her.
  • 盖经三年，产六男，六女 - 盖经三年 means after about three years, and 产六男，六女 means gave birth to six sons and six daughters.
  • 盘瓠死，后自相配偶，因为夫妇 - 盘瓠死 means the dog died, and 后自相配偶，因为夫妇 means later they paired off and became couples.
  • 织绩木皮，染以草实 - 织绩 means wove, 木皮 means tree bark, and 染以草实 means dyeing with plant seeds.
  • 好五色衣服，裁制皆有尾形 - 好五色衣服 means liked colorful clothes, and 裁制皆有尾形 means cut and made all with tail shapes.
  • 后母归，以语王，王遣使迎诸男女，天不复两 - 后母归，以语王 means the mother returned and told the king, and 王遣使迎诸男女，天不复两 means the king sent envoys to bring back all the men and women, and the heavens no longer separated them.
  • 衣服褊裢，言语侏(人离)，饮食蹲踞，好山恶都 - 衣服褊裢 means clothes were tight-fitting, 言语侏(人离) means speech was short and simple, 饮食蹲踞 means ate and sat in a squatting position, and 好山恶都 means liked mountains and disliked cities.
  • 王顺其意，赐以名山，广泽，号曰蛮夷 - 王顺其意 means the king followed their wishes, and 赐以名山，广泽，号曰蛮夷 means gave them famous mountains, vast lakes, and called them the barbarians.
  • 蛮夷者，外痴内黠，安土重旧，以其受异气于天命，故待以不常之律 - 外痴内黠 means seem simple on the outside but clever inside, 安土重旧 means loved their land and valued tradition, and 以其受异气于天命，故待以不常之律 means because they received a different breath from heaven, they were treated with special laws.
  • 田作，贾贩，无关繻，符传，租税之赋 - 田作，贾贩 means farming and trading, and 无关繻，符传，租税之赋 means without customs barriers, official documents, or taxes.
  • 有邑，君长皆赐印绶 - 有邑 means had settlements, and 君长皆赐印绶 means chiefs were given seals and ribbons.
  • 冠用獭皮，取其游食于水 - 冠用獭皮 means wore hats made of otter fur, and 取其游食于水 means because they lived by fishing in the water.
  • 今即梁汉、巴蜀、武陵、长沙、庐江郡夷是也 - 今即 means now these are, and 梁汉、巴蜀、武陵、长沙、庐江郡夷 refers to specific regions inhabited by these people.
  • 用糁，杂鱼肉，叩槽而号，以祭盘瓠，其俗至今 - 用糁，杂鱼肉 means used millet and mixed with fish and meat, 叩槽而号 means beat the trough and call out, and 以祭盘瓠，其俗至今 means to worship the dog, and this custom continues to this day.
  • 故世称“赤髀，横裙，盘瓠子孙” - 故世称 means therefore, the world calls, 赤髀，横裙 means red legs and wide skirts, and 盘瓠子孙 means descendants of the dog.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 盘瓠 - A mythological dog that saved a kingdom and was rewarded with a princess.
  • 蛮夷 - Refers to non-Han Chinese ethnic groups, often seen as less civilized.
  • 梁汉、巴蜀、武陵、长沙、庐江郡 - Specific regions in ancient China.
  • 用糁，杂鱼肉，叩槽而号，以祭盘瓠 - Describes a ritual practice involving food offerings and a call to honor the dog.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 颖乃还眠向寐处 - Ying returns to sleep in the place where he was about to sleep. Sets the scene for the dream.
  • 梦见谓颖曰 - In a dream, to say to Ying. Introduces the dream narrative.
  • 我以穷苦告君，奈何不相愍悼乎 - To inform you of my poverty and suffering; why don't you pity me? Rhetorical question expressing emotional appeal.
  • 子为谁 - Who are you? Polite inquiry.
  • 吾本赵人，今属汪芒氏之神 - I was originally from Zhao; now I belong to the god of the Wamang people. Supernatural element.
  • 子棺今何所在 - Where is your coffin now? Direct question about location.
  • 近在君帐北十数步水侧枯杨树下，即是吾也 - Near your tent, northward, a dozen steps, by the water, under a dead willow tree; that is me. Detailed description of location.
  • 天将明，不复得见，君必念之 - It will soon be dawn; I won't be able to see you again; you must remember me. Conditional statement with urgency.
  • 忽然便寤 - Suddenly woke up. Transition from dream to reality.
  • 虽曰梦不足怪，此何太适 - Even though it's a dream, it's not unusual; why is it so strange? Rhetorical question introducing skepticism.
  • 左右曰：“亦何惜须臾，不验之耶？” - Those around him said: Why not spend a little time to verify it? Logical suggestion.
  • 颖即起，率十数人将导顺水上 - Ying immediately got up; led a dozen people upstream. Action with vague number of people.
  • 果得一枯杨 - Indeed found a dead willow tree. Discovery is straightforward.
  • 是矣 - That's it. Confirmation.
  • 掘其下，未几，果得棺 - Dug underneath; in a short while, indeed found the coffin. Action with brief time frame.
  • 棺甚朽坏，没半水中 - The coffin was very rotten; half submerged in water. Vivid description.
  • 向闻于人，谓之虚矣；世俗所传，不可无验 - What I heard from others; they said it was false; what is passed down by the common people cannot be without verification. Reflects belief in verification.
  • 为移其棺，葬之而去 - To move the coffin; buried it and left. Final action.

  • 敞乃驰还，遣吏捕捉，拷问，具服 - Formal expression meaning 'the official then returned quickly, sent officials to capture the perpetrators, interrogated them, and they confessed.'

  • 年六十 - Literally 'sixty years old,' a common way to specify age in Classical Chinese.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 乃分袂泣别 - Compound expression meaning 'they parted with tears' or 'they separated with weeping'; *分袂* is a classical idiom for parting, and *泣别* means to part in tears.
  • 青衣 - Term meaning 'a person dressed in green' or more specifically, a female servant or maid in classical Chinese literature, denoting a low-status attendant.
  • 未逾数步 - Literally 'not having gone more than a few steps,' used to convey that a short distance was covered before something unexpected occurred.
  • 不见舍宇 - 'Did not see a house' or 'no house was visible,' indicating that the scene had changed dramatically.
  • 惟有一冢 - 'Only a mound' or 'only a grave,' indicating that the location had transformed into a burial site.
  • 度当时荒忙出走 - 'Du was in a state of panic and fled hastily at the time,' indicating a sudden and chaotic departure.

  • 诘度何处得来？ - 'She questioned Du about where he had obtained it,' using *诘* for 'questioned' and *何处得来* as a standard inquiry about the source.
  • 度具以告 - 'Du fully explained it to her,' with *具以告* being a formal expression for 'fully explained.'
  • 妃闻，悲泣不能自胜 - 'The queen heard and wept bitterly, unable to control herself,' with *不能自胜* meaning 'unable to restrain oneself.'
  • 向疑耳 - 'It was merely a suspicion before,' indicating that the queen had doubts but not yet confirmation.
  • 遣人发冢启柩视之 - 'She sent people to open the tomb and examine the coffin,' with *发冢* meaning 'to open a grave' and *启柩* meaning 'to open the coffin.'
  • 原葬悉在，唯不见枕 - 'All the original burial items were there, except the pillow was missing,' indicating that the pillow was not found in the tomb.
  • 解体看之，交情宛若 - 'She examined the body and found that the relationship was as real as ever,' with *解体* meaning 'to examine the body' and *交情宛若* meaning 'the affection was as real as ever.'
  • 秦妃始信之 - 'The Queen of Qin finally believed him,' with *始信之* meaning 'began to believe it.'
  • 叹曰 - 'She sighed and said,' a common expression in classical narrative.
  • 我女大圣，死经二十三年，犹能与生人交往 - 'My daughter, a great sage, died 23 years ago, yet she could still interact with the living,' a key line affirming the supernatural nature of the story.
  • 此是我真女婿也 - 'This is my true son-in-law,' indicating the queen's recognition of Du as her daughter's husband.
  • 遂封度为驸马都尉 - 'Thus, she conferred upon Du the title of *驸马都尉*,' a formal title for a prince’s son-in-law.
  • 赐金帛车马，令还本国 - 'She granted him gold, silk, carriages, and horses, and ordered him to return to his native country.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秦国 - Refers to the ancient Chinese state of Qin, one of the major states during the Warring States period (475–221 BCE), which eventually unified China under Qin Shi Huang.
  • 秦妃 - Likely refers to the wife or mother of a ruler of the state of Qin, though no specific historical queen is named in the text. The term *妃* can mean queen, concubine, or wife, depending on context.
  • 驸马都尉 - An official title granted to a prince’s son-in-law in ancient China. The term *驸马* (Fu Ma) originally meant 'a horse attached to a prince’s chariot,' but later became the title for a royal son-in-law. This passage explains the etymology of the term.
  • 金枕 - A symbolic object in the story, representing the supernatural connection between the deceased princess and her husband, and the key to verifying Du’s identity.
  • 冢 - Refers to a burial mound or grave, common in ancient Chinese funerary practices.
  • 解体看之 - A reference to examining the body of the deceased, which is a common element in Chinese ghost stories and supernatural narratives.
  • 交情宛若 - Indicates the emotional or spiritual connection between the deceased and the living, a common theme in Chinese literature involving ghosts and spirits.

Proper Nouns

  • 度 - A personal name, likely a fictional or historical figure in the story.
  • 秦国 - A proper noun referring to the ancient Chinese state.
  • 秦妃 - A title referring to a member of the royal family of the State of Qin.
  • 驸马都尉 - A title, not a person.
  • 驸马 - A title, also not a person, but a term used to describe a royal son-in-law.

  • 字符常 - Zhong You's courtesy name, a common practice in ancient China to show respect and formality.
  • 鬼物 - Refers to supernatural beings or ghosts, a common theme in Chinese folklore and literature.
  • 朝会 - Refers to court meetings or assemblies, important social and political gatherings in ancient China.
  • 裲裆 - Type of armor or protective clothing, often used in ancient Chinese military and ceremonial contexts.
  • 白练衫 - White silk robe, a type of garment often associated with elegance and purity in Chinese culture.
  • 丹绣 - Red embroidery, a decorative technique often used to signify status or beauty.

Proper Nouns

  • 颍川 - Region of Yingchuan.

  • 字符常 - Zhong You's courtesy name.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 搜神記 - Classical Chinese text on supernatural tales; often translated as 'Records of the Supernatural' or 'A Record of Searching for Gods.'
  • 陳國 - Ancient state of Chen, located in present-day Henan Province.
  • 京兆尹 - High-ranking official in charge of the capital region during the Han Dynasty.
  • 左氏傳 - Commentary on the *Zuo Zhuan*, an ancient Chinese historical text.
  • 鬼物 - Spirits or supernatural beings.
  • 揚言 - To speak out or proclaim.
  • 不借 - Literally 'not borrow,' but in this context, likely refers to a specific object or item.
  • 楮 - Paper made from the bark of the mulberry tree.
  • 衰绖 - Mourning attire.
  • 妖物 - Supernatural or malevolent entities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The ancient state of Chen, which existed during the Zhou Dynasty and later became part of the Han Dynasty.
  • A significant administrative position in the Han Dynasty, responsible for the capital region.
  • A well-known commentary on the *Zuo Zhuan*, which is one of the Three Commentaries of the Spring and Autumn Annals.
  • Reflects the belief in spirits and the supernatural prevalent in ancient Chinese culture.
  • Indicates the presence of malevolent or mischievous supernatural entities.

  • 張漢直 - Main character, Zhang Hanzhi.
